entries.title: DePaul Prep relishes the road, dominates New Trier in statement sectional semi win
entries.summary: <p>Loyola’s gym is too small to host a Class 4A sectional, but there are positives for fans that manage to snag a ticket. The sectional environment is always the best part of the season around the area, but in a smaller gym the intensity goes up several notches. Fans were standing in rows five deep around the upper level of the gym in Wilmette.</p><p>As No. 2 DePaul Prep took the court on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/high-school-basketball/scores/2026/03/03/ihsa-state-basketball-playoff-scores-illinois" >Tuesday</a>, boos rained down. And Rashaun Porter smiled.</p><p>“There is something about away games,” Porter said. “It just brings more energy when the crowd is against you, everybody looking at you as the underdog. We had our section, but it felt like 95% of it was New Trier fans.”</p><p>Porter and his teammates turned in one of the best performances the area has seen this season in a big game, dominating No. 10 New Trier 56-38.</p><p>“That is a talented team,” Trevians coach Scott Fricke said. “Our plan was good. We started the game kind of locked in defensively. But once the game gets going, they have so many gus that can make plays. Just very difficult.”</p><p>New Trier junior Matthew Logue scored with 5:15 left in the third quarter to pull the Trevians within 27-22 but they didn’t score again the rest of the quarter. DePaul Prep led 34-22 heading into the fourth.</p><p>“Maybe our experience showed,” Rams point guard AJ Chambers said. “It was tense going into it. We know that Danny [Houlihan] and Chris [Kirkpatrick] are killers. We just played together.”</p><p>Chambers finished with 10 points, and his overall defensive toughness set the tone on the perimeter. He had a running steal for an easy layup in the first half and then just ripped the ball out of a New Trier guard’s hands in the second half for another score.</p><p>“This is a high-pressure game in Wilmette,” DePaul Prep coach Tom Kleinschmidt said. “We knew the crowd would be against us. We had two good days of practice. We were patient in the zone to get layups and we valued the ball.”</p><p>The Rams had just six turnovers and outrebounding New Trier 23-16.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>DePaul Prep’s Rykan Woo (4) moves the ball against New Trier’s Christopher Kirkpatrick (4) during the game at Loyola.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Kirsten Stickney/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“We didn’t do a great job of rebounding, and we’ve outrebounded teams all season,” Fricke said. “A lot of it had to do with [DePaul]. They are so athletic and big and strong.”</p><p>Porter, a 6-7 Toledo recruit, led the Rams (30-3) with 16 points and six rebounds. Senior guard Rykan Woo scored 16 and Zion Lee added 11 points and six rebounds off the bench.</p><p>Most observers expected a close game, and the near blowout score will open eyes around the state. But Porter believes the Rams can hit an even higher level of play.</p><p>“We have some things we can improve on,” Porter said. “This was probably a six or a seven out of ten for us. I love this team. It is all unselfishness. No one is worried about scoring.”</p><p>Logue led New Trier (26-8) with 12 points and six rebounds. Kirkpatrick finished with 11 points.</p><p>DePaul Prep will face the winner of Wednesday’s Loyola vs. Evanston semifinal at Loyola on Friday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="zxx" dir="ltr"><a href="https://t.co/UyqFwocj63">pic.twitter.com/UyqFwocj63</a></p>— Michael O'Brien (@michaelsobrien) <a href="https://twitter.com/michaelsobrien/status/2028997730836660687?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 4, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>

entries.title: Driver fatally strikes pedestrian in North Park
entries.summary: <p>A driver fatally struck a woman Tuesday night in the North Park neighborhood, according to police.</p><p>A 70-year-old man driving westbound in a Chevy SUV hit the woman who was "near the street" about 6:15 p.m. in the 4000 block of West Foster Avenue, Chicago police said in a statement.</p><p>The woman, whose age wasn’t known, was taken to St. Francis Hospital, where she was pronounced dead, police said.</p><p>CPD’s Major Accidents Unit is investigating the crash, police said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Bulls still hopeful guard Jaden Ivey can return this season
entries.summary: <p>Guard <b>Jaden Ivey </b>(left knee) won’t travel with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" target="_blank" >Bulls</a> on their upcoming five-game trip. Coach <b>Billy Donovan </b>said Ivey has made good progress, but the medical team wants to keep him home.</p><p>Donovan also sounded confident Ivey would play again this season. Right now, the Bulls are working to get Ivey’s knee to calm down and to make sure he has enough strength in his quad to accelerate and decelerate.</p><p>‘‘When you’re out there playing and you’ve got fatigue and are dealing with a sore knee and you’ve got some strength deficiency, then you’re putting yourself in harm’s way,’’ Donovan said.</p><p>After the Bulls acquired Ivey in a trade Feb. 3 with the Pistons, his lack of explosiveness was obvious, especially compared to his form before he suffered a broken left leg in January 2025 and an injury to his right knee during the preseason. After not playing in the Bulls’ loss Feb. 19 to the Raptors, Ivey declared the ‘‘old J.I. is dead.’’ He was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/02/21/bulls-and-pistons-confident-jaden-ivey-come-back-from-injury" target="_blank" >shut down two days later</a>.</p><p>Guard <b>Anfernee Simons </b>(left wrist) and forwards <b>Jalen Smith </b>(right calf) and <b>Patrick Williams </b>(right quadriceps) will travel. Donovan said that Williams and Smith might return at some point during the trip but that Simons still is dealing with some discomfort in the hand.</p><h3>Miller time</h3><p>One positive about the Bulls’ injury plight is that it gives some players a chance to impress in relatively low-stakes situations.</p><p>Forward <b>Leonard Miller </b>is someone who is taking advantage of that opportunity.</p><p>‘‘I’m trying to do that right now,’’ said Miller, who was one of the first Bulls off the bench Tuesday against the Thunder. ‘‘I feel like so far I have been, and I’m just going to continue to build.’’</p><p>Acquired from the Timberwolves as part of the <b>Ayo Dosunmu </b>trade, Miller scored a career-high 15 points in the Bulls’ victory Sunday against the Bucks. That followed his outing Thursday against the Trail Blazers in which he scored 11 to reach double figures for the first time in his career.</p><p>Donovan said Miller has a strong feel for how to play, has good length and passes and shoots well. He also said the Bulls have gotten ‘‘really good minutes’’ from him the last couple of games.</p><p>‘‘He impacts the game [in] a lot of different ways,’’ Donovan said. ‘‘He’s on the glass, he’s around the basket and he can make a [three-pointer]. He can put it down.’’</p><p>Miller, 22, was a second-round pick in the 2023 draft and played 49 games in parts of three seasons with the Timberwolves.</p><h3>Distant but familiar</h3><p>Guard <b>Josh Giddey </b>began his NBA career with the Thunder, selected sixth overall in the 2021 draft. Despite his obvious potential, the Thunder dealt him to the Bulls for guard <b>Alex Caruso</b>, who proved to be a key piece of their 2025 title team.</p><p>Thunder coach <b>Mark Daigneault</b> is too busy with his own team to watch much of Giddey. From what he has seen, however, he said Giddey’s shooting has improved and his body has matured since the trade.</p><p>‘‘He was a guy that had a very high baseline, obviously, with us and also showed the capacity to improve,’’ Daigneault said. ‘‘Usually, the biggest predictor of future improvement is past improvement, and he’s done that.’’</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: Chicago Fire breaks ground on $750 million stadium at The 78
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Fire broke ground Tuesday on a $750 million soccer stadium in the South Loop, which was also a first step toward developing the long-dormant parcel known as The 78.</p><p>The 22,000-seat stadium, bankrolled by the Fire’s billionaire owner, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/06/03/chicago-fire-soccer-stadium-south-loop-the-78-joe-mansueto-related-midwest" >Joe Mansueto</a>, is expected to be complete in time for the team’s 2028-29 season. The soccer stadium will be a huge boon for the team, which currently plays at Soldier Field but has had games pushed to the suburbs due to scheduling conflicts with the Chicago Bears and concerts.</p><p>“This is a culmination of some work that seeks to turn some of the most valuable and prominent pieces of fallow land in the city of Chicago into a world-class stadium and a vibrant surrounding community,” Ald. Pat Dowell (3rd), whose ward includes the project, said.</p><p>The stadium will serve as an anchor for The 78, the 62-acre site at Roosevelt Road and Clark Street that has eluded development for years. The land, owned by Related Midwest, is one of the largest undeveloped parcels left in the city.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/fire/2025/09/18/chicago-fire-stadium-the78-plan-commission-approvals" >first phase</a> of development at The 78 will include more than 1,400 feet of publicly-accessible riverfront space, a new water taxi stop, 1 1/2 miles of bike trails, Divvy bike stations, two temporary surface parking lots, public parking and 3 acres of sports fields, according to Related Midwest.</p><p>Once the 62-acre project is complete, it's estimated to generate $8 billion in economic impact. The development will also include homes, trails and a connection to Ping Tom Memorial Park.</p><p>“This not only will be a great place to come watch a soccer match or concert, it'll be a fantastic place to call home for thousands of Chicagoans,” Dave Baldwin, Chicago Fire’s president of business operations, said.</p><p>Mansueto had two options when selecting the team's permanent home: The 78 and the now-defunct Lincoln Yards megadevelopment. Lincoln Yards collapsed last year and a large portion of the North Side project was purchased by Chicago-based JDL Development, who received the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/02/18/chicago-city-council-foundry-park-successor-failed-lincoln-yards-project-north-side" >City Council’s approval</a> last month for a scaled-back vision for the site.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9f0000" name="image-9f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Chicago Fire owner and Chairman Joe Mansueto speaks during the team's stadium groundbreaking at The 78." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8140cc6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7049x3956+0+183/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd6%2F446b4aa147ebac38d07873de540f%2Ffirestadium-260304-021.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dd2d086/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7049x3956+0+183/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd6%2F446b4aa147ebac38d07873de540f%2Ffirestadium-260304-021.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8140cc6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7049x3956+0+183/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd6%2F446b4aa147ebac38d07873de540f%2Ffirestadium-260304-021.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Fire owner and Chairman Joe Mansueto speaks during the team’s stadium groundbreaking at The 78.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Mansueto said he “immediately fell in love” with The 78 and its picturesque views of Chicago. And a soccer stadium was already a permitted use at the site.</p><p>“I knew it was the right home for the Fire,” he said. “It's really perfect. I think the right model today to build a neighborhood is to have a stadium as an anchor. So we're very proud to be the anchor to The 78 site and really get this site going.”</p><p>The land, once owned by convicted political fixer <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2019/4/5/18355382/trump-insider-wanted-to-develop-s-loop-site-but-couldn-t-strike-deal-with-rezko" >Tony Rezko</a>, had been considered for Amazon’s second world headquarters. It was also in the running to become United Airlines' new corporate headquarters, the new home for JPMorganChase and the spot for a casino. The University of Illinois in 2024 pulled out of plans to build its $300 million technology and research hub <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/02/10/university-illinois-wacker-drive-office-building-dpi-tech-research-hub" >Discovery Partners Institute</a>, instead picking the Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park and buying a building in the Loop. </p><p>Renderings, released in June, show a stadium that resembles a warehouse, invoking Chicago’s industrial manufacturing roots, with fans entering from Roosevelt Road.</p><p>The stadium and its surrounding districts are designed to be a year-round economic engine. Baldwin said events from rugby and lacrosse games to concerts and corporate events will be held at the stadium.</p><p>“We expect the Chicago Fire stadium to be an anchor on The 78 that will attract recreation, dining, cultural spaces and investments for our entire city,” Mayor Brandon Johnson said. </p><p>He also made a slight dig at the Chicago Bears, who have created a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/02/26/chicago-bears-property-tax-incentives-illinois-house-city-opposition-arlington-heights-proposed-stadium" >bidding war</a> between Illinois and Indiana over which state can claim the NFL franchise.</p><p>“I will say that it is so good to make sure at least one of our football teams in Chicago is good,” Johnson said. “One more to go!”</p><p>The Fire stadium gained <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/fire/2025/09/25/chicago-fire-city-council-approval-south-loop-stadium-the-78" >instant momentum </a>and a speedy approval because Mansueto is footing the bill. The development has also received praise from South Loop residents, though some in Chinatown and other nearby neighborhoods have expressed concerns over the development <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/07/30/chinatown-chicago-fire-stadium" >pricing out long-time residents</a> and small businesses.</p><p>As construction begins on the stadium, the future remains hazy for the White Sox, whose chairman, Jerry Reinsdorf, once eyed The 78 as a potential spot for a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2024/1/17/24042048/white-sox-new-stadium-78-site-south-loop-related-midwest-reinsdorf" >new stadium</a>. The land’s development agreement doesn't allow for a second stadium, unless the developers come back to the Chicago Plan Commission and the community for consideration.</p><div class="Enhancement" entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Fire break ground on new stadium, take lead on city peers and move closer to igniting Chicago soccer
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/fire" >Fire</a> have spent much of their existence looking for a new home. Now they’re ahead of other Chicago teams in the stadium game.</p><p>On Tuesday, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/fire/2026/02/24/fire-hosting-tuesday-groundbreaking-ceremony-for-new-stadium" >Fire broke ground</a> on their new arena in The 78. The $750 million privately funded facility is expected to open in late summer 2028. </p><p>“This isn’t just a stadium, it’s a statement of ambition,” Fire owner and chairman Joe Mansueto said during his speech. “It’s about pride. It’s about believing in the city of Chicago.”</p><p>Stadium intrigue has been one of the biggest stories around Chicago sports over the past few years.</p><p>Dissatisfied with Rate Field, the White Sox released renderings for a new stadium at The 78. Owner Jerry Reinsdorf even<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2023/12/6/23990963/white-sox-chairman-jerry-reinsdorf-meets-with-nashville-mayor" > met with Nashville mayor Freddie O’Connell </a>during the 2023 Winter Meetings.</p><p>The Bears, currently the Fire’s roommate at Soldier Field, are in the midst of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium" >a quixotic quest for a new home</a> that could end up in Arlington Heights or Northwest Indiana. Mayor Brandon Johnson kept Chicago itself in the picture, reiterating Tuesday that the Bears belong within city limits.</p><p>Meanwhile, the Fire are two years away from their own field on a desired plot of land. And nothing in the Fire’s history would indicate they’d be in this spot compared to some of their Chicago peers.</p><p>In 2006, the Fire thought they had found their long-term home when they moved to suburban Bridgeview. The soccer-specific venue offered intimate sightlines and gave the Fire the first choice of dates for matches.</p><p>Though the facility remained a capable host to soccer, by the end of the 2010s it was clear the Fire were looking to exit. The arena had fallen behind newer grounds, and the team’s on-field decline paired with Bridgeview’s location meant attendance plummeted.</p><p>In late 2019, Mansueto announced he was moving the Fire back to Soldier Field. Observers predicted it was a temporary solution and not a permanent answer because of inevitable issues with field conditions, choice of dates and creating a soccer atmosphere.</p><p>Those problems should be a thing of the past now for the Fire, and the new building could potentially spark a market that’s been underserved by its team. Despite the city’s strong soccer history, whether it’s the old North American Soccer League’s Sting or a flourishing youth and amateur scene, the Fire haven’t been one of MLS’ flagship clubs for over 15 years.</p><p>MLS commissioner Don Garber said, around six weeks ago, he drove around the city with Mansueto and visited the team’s new training center. The visit showed Garber again that Chicago is a soccer town.</p><p>“The Chicago Fire have had a small but rabid fan base, particularly among their supporters, and they’ve been missing something,” Garber said. “[Mansueto] mentioned it when he talked about his vision. He believes in the city, he believes in this team, believes in the sport, and believes that if he can deliver a best-in-class, world-class soccer stadium, it’ll connect all the dots and give this city what it is that I think they want.</p><p>“They want to be a leading city. They’re tired of hearing about other cities that have proven that they can really move the needle in the United States. This city and its fans want to move the needle and I am highly confident that they’ll be able to do that.”</p><p>The Fire are in the lead in another category: building a new stadium in the city.</p><p></p><p></p><p></p>
entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: White Sox' Jordan Hicks back in bullpen after rough go in Giants' rotation
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — Right-hander <b>Jordan Hicks </b>is trying to figure it out with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> this spring. A starter last season with the Giants, he’s now falling back on his bullpen roots with the Cardinals.</p><p>Hicks had a miserable time starting for the Giants. He made nine starts and was 1-5 with a 6.95 ERA before being sent back to the bullpen. He eventually was traded to the Red Sox as part of the blockbuster deal that sent slugger <b>Rafael Devers </b>to the Giants.</p><p>On Feb. 1, the Red Sox dumped him on the Sox for a player to be named and a minor-leaguer. At 29, he’s a reclamation project, to say the least.</p><p>Asked by the Sun-Times on Tuesday whether he rather would start or relieve, Hicks shrugged.</p><p>‘‘At this point, I got my shot [to start],’’ Hicks said. ‘‘I just want to do what’s best for the team at the moment. If they want me back in the bullpen, then that’s what I’ll do.’’</p><p>In his salad days, Hicks used his 100 mph fastball to notch 28 saves in five seasons for the Cardinals. Sox manager <b>Will Venable </b>confirmed Hicks is ticketed for the bullpen, and Hicks said he would like to rediscover some of that magic.</p><p>‘‘I had a tough year last year,’’ he said. ‘‘Obviously, I wish I had gone about some things differently. I’m taking care of my body now. I wish I had come to this place one year earlier. But it is what it is. I’m just trying to be the best version of myself moving forward. I’m happy doing both.’’</p><h3>Acuna getting acclimated</h3><p>New center fielder <b>Luisangel Acuna </b>said through an interpreter that he’s beginning to feel comfortable in his new environment. Acuna was the key acquisition in the trade Jan. 20 that sent center fielder <b>Luis Robert Jr.</b> to the Mets.</p><p>Acuna played in the Sox’ Cactus League loss Monday to the Giants and went 2-for-3 with a home run in the fifth inning while batting second. On Tuesday, he was on the back fields playing in a ‘‘B’’ game.</p><p>‘‘I just want to have a good spring training and stay healthy,’’ he said. ‘‘Work on my offense and my defense.’’</p><p>About playing center, he said: ‘‘I’m very happy about that. I’m very comfortable out there. I played there in Venezuela and feel like it’s the position where I’ll be my best.’’</p><h3>Newcomb stretching it out</h3><p>Left-hander <b>Sean Newcomb </b>is one of nine pitchers Venable is stretching out in anticipation of choosing a five-man starting rotation before spring training finishes.</p><p>Newcomb, 32, was a reliever for the Athletics last season and didn’t make a start in 36 appearances. He made his second start of the spring Tuesday, this one against the Padres.</p><p>Does he prefer to start or relieve? A nine-year veteran now with his sixth team, Newcomb has been primarily a reliever since making a combined 49 starts in his first two seasons with the Braves.</p><p>‘‘I just want the ball, it really doesn’t matter,’’ Newcomb said after throwing 54 pitches in three innings. ‘‘The aspect of starting I like is the routine and the certainty. But there are pluses and minuses to both.’’</p><h3>Padres 4, Sox 3</h3><p>Sox starter <b>Sean Newcomb </b>allowed two runs and five hits, including a home run to <b>Nick Castellanos</b>, with a walk and a strikeout in three innings Tuesday.</p><p>‘‘It’s good to get through a little bit of everything,’’ Newcomb said about his outing. ‘‘I let up the homer there, so that was good to get out of the way.’’</p><p>• Pitcher <b>Wikelman Gonzalez </b>left during the seventh inning with tightness in his lower back. His status is day-to-day.</p><p>• Third baseman <b>Miguel Vargas </b>went 2-for-3 with a homer in the third inning against Padres starter <b>Nick Pivetta</b>.</p><p>• The Sox don’t have a game this spring against any of the World Baseball Classic teams, which played all over Arizona on Tuesday and will again Wednesday. The Dodgers host Mexico on Wednesday night at Camelback Ranch. The game is sold out.</p><p>• <b>On deck</b>: The Sox have the day off Wednesday. They will play the Guardians at 7 p.m. Thursday in Glendale.</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" entries.title: Probation offered to Chicago woman accused of killing abusive boyfriend — but her lawyers want case dropped
entries.summary: <p>A Chicago woman accused of killing her abusive boyfriend left court in tears Tuesday after prosecutors refused to drop the charges and instead offered a plea deal that would require her to serve probation.</p><p>Keshia Golden was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/10/25/23423307/2m-bail-woman-accused-fatally-stabbing-boyfriend-argument-over-microwave-use" target="_blank" >charged with first-degree murder</a> in the 2022 stabbing of Calvin Sidney. Golden’s lawyers have argued that she stabbed Sidney in the leg when he attacked her after their baby shower.</p><p>During Tuesday’s hearing, Cook County prosecutors offered Golden a deal to plead to lesser charges of second-degree murder in exchange for two years of probation — a conviction her lawyers said would follow her for life.</p><p>The plea deal remains on the table, but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/11/4/23441087/pregnant-woman-murder-charge-community-group-provides-bond-keshia-golden" target="_blank" >Golden’s lawyers and supporters</a> continue to call on Cook County State’s Attorney Eileen O’Neill Burke to dismiss the case before it goes to trial. </p><p>“We expect State's Attorney [O’Neill] Burke to do the right thing here,” said Julie Koehler, one of Golden’s public defenders. “She has stated numerous times that she helps victims of domestic violence. … Well, here's one. Here's a victim of domestic violence that she needs to help.”</p><p>The state’s attorney’s office said the case remains pending and “continues through the judicial process.”</p><p>On Oct. 22, 2022, Golden and Sidney were hosting the shower for their first child together. After the party, Sidney slammed Golden into a kitchen counter and a fridge, according to her lawyers. A relative tried to separate them, but Sidney wouldn’t let go, so <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/10/30/23429205/calvin-sydney-dead-pregnant-woman-charged-murder-father-keshia-golden-domestic-violence" target="_blank" >Golden grabbed a knife and stabbed him</a> in his leg, her lawyers said.</p><p>He was hit in his femoral artery and later died at Mount Sinai Hospital, according to officials.</p><p>Golden’s attorneys have argued she was acting in self-defense against a man who had a documented history of domestic violence.</p><p>Golden has been out of jail while awaiting trial, thanks to her team of lawyers and advocates. She was able to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/24/chicago-crime-keshia-golden-domestic-violence-abuse-murder" target="_blank" >give birth and raise her daughter</a>, Ky’liyah.</p><p>“She has done everything over the past three years that they have asked,” Koehler said of her client. “What more do they want from this woman? And what more does a woman have to do to protect herself from a man who is slamming her head into a kitchen counter?”</p><p>Golden is expected back in court April 7.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/02/24/chicago-crime-keshia-golden-domestic-violence-abuse-murder" target="_blank" >Chicago woman says she killed abusive boyfriend to save herself and their unborn child. Will a jury agree?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/11/4/23441087/pregnant-woman-murder-charge-community-group-provides-bond-keshia-golden" target="_blank" >Pregnant woman facing murder charge to be released from jail after community group provides bond</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/11/3/23439285/citing-state-law-judge-reduces-bail-for-pregnant-woman-facing-murder-charge" target="_blank" >Citing state law, judge reduces bail for pregnant woman facing murder charge</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>

entries.title: Horoscope for Wednesday, March 04, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>Avoid shopping (except food and gas) or major decisions from 8:30 a.m. to 1:15 p.m. After that, the moon moves from Virgo into Libra.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a feel-good day! You have a warm feeling in your tummy. However, you might feel mildly stressed, or perhaps excited. Slow down and take a moment to catch your breath. Figure out what's nagging you in the back of your mind.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> A friend or a member of a group might throw you a curveball today. Be ready for this. Or perhaps you will meet a real character today — someone intriguing and different. Whatever happens, give yourself a moment to process things before you respond.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a tricky day because authority figures might surprise you. ("Busted!") Fortunately, you look good in their eyes, which will help you deal with the unexpected. Whatever happens, don't respond with a knee-jerk reaction. Take a minute to think about your best interests.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Travel plans might change today. Similarly, school schedules might also change. These changes might also impact publishing, medicine and the law. They could be significant, or they might be as minor as meeting someone new from another culture or far away.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Keep an eye on your banking and anything to do with shared property, taxes and debt because something unexpected could impact these areas. It might be something important you don't want to overlook. (You snooze — you lose.) Or an unexpected favor or gift that comes your way. Yeah!</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today a partner, spouse or close friend will surprise you in some way. They might say or do something you least expect, or they might introduce you to someone who is unusual and exciting. Stay flexible and keep an open attitude. "Qui sait?"</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Something pleasant related to your job, medical work, or a pet might please you today. Someone might do a favor for you, or you might receive an unexpected gift or help from someone. Pet owners would be wise to keep an eye on their pets.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Because unexpected things could happen today, this is an interesting day for you. Unexpected invitations might come your way, your kids might surprise you in some fashion, and sports events, the arts, the theater, and social outings might thrill you. New romance is possible!</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Your home routine might be interrupted today. You might buy something high-tech or exciting. A gift from a family member might delight you. A real estate opportunity might present itself. Stock the fridge because you might spontaneously entertain.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This could be an exciting day because you might meet new faces and see new places. You might also come up with an exciting — even brilliant — idea. Your daily routine might change. This is why you need to be on your toes and aware of what's going on.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This might be a bountiful day for you. Gifts, money or financial advantages might come your way unexpectedly. Admittedly, the reverse might happen, and you might spontaneously buy yourself something that pleases you. You might get a better-paying job, a raise or praise.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a perfect day to seek an exciting break from your daily routine. New friends who are fascinating and interesting might appear. You might suddenly socialize with someone or attend a party — or you buy yourself something exciting to wear. (We like.)</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Catherine O'Hara (1954-2026) shares your birthday today. You are gentle, sensitive and forever curious. You have a rich imagination and great powers of concentration. This year you will reap the benefits of your previous hard work. Kudos, awards and acknowledgment will be yours. You might attain power and leadership. Expect the spotlight to shine on you. </p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: As WBC fever takes hold, some Cubs also have 2028 Olympics on their minds
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — The mere mention of competing in the 2028 Olympics gave <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a> left-hander Matthew Boyd chills.</p><p>Would he be interested?</p><p>“Without a doubt,” Boyd told the Sun-Times on Tuesday. “It’s two years away, and I will be 37, but I believe my best baseball is still ahead of me. And if that opportunity presents itself, it’s something I would not hesitate to do.”</p><p>International baseball was already in the air as exhibition games began Tuesday in preparation for the World Baseball Classic.</p><p>The Cubs trio of Boyd, third baseman Alex Bregman and outfielder Pete Crow-Armstrong contributed to Team USA’s shellacking of the Giants in nearby Scottsdale. Video clips of slugger Seiya Suzuki launching a moon shot in Tokyo for Team Japan made the rounds on social media.</p><p>In all, the Cubs — who lost their exhibition game against Team Italy 9-4 — have eight players from their 40-man roster participating in the WBC, including catcher Miguel Amaya (Panama) and pitchers Javier Assad (Mexico), Daniel Palencia (Venezuela) and Jameson Taillon (Canada).</p><p>Buzz for this year’s tournament is building as a result of increased buy-in from star players and the memory of a Hollywood ending in 2023, when Team Japan defeated Team USA in the final. MLB has figured out how to navigate the demands of the WBC while also preparing for the regular season.</p><p>Will the excitement translate to the 2028 Summer Games?</p><p>First, MLB and its players’ union would need to strike an agreement to allow major-leaguers to participate in the Olympics.</p><p>“I think that we’re a lot closer to [an agreement] than the last time we talked about it,” commissioner Rob Manfred told reporters at the owners meetings last month. “There are some issues with the [players’ association] that we just need to resolve. I sense a lot of momentum towards playing in L.A. in 2028. I think we’re going to get over those issues.”</p><p>With an agreement in place, it would be up to individual players, in communication with their teams, to decide whether they could commit to the Olympic tournament at Dodger Stadium, scheduled for July 13-19, 2028.</p><p>“We support the league, we support its initiatives and we support the growth of the game,” Cubs general manager Carter Hawkins said. “So if that’s the way we go, I’m sure, as a fan, people will be excited to see that, and we’ll be supportive in every way we can.”</p><p>It would be a rare opportunity for players. Baseball has only been included in the Olympics once since 2008, revived in 2021 in Tokyo. The timing — overlapping with the All-Star break around midseason — will make some players think twice.</p><p>“Everyone’s different,” Amaya said. “And sometimes, if you do it from your soul and the pride of your country, not how you’re feeling [physically], you’re going to shake your body and set your mind and say, ‘OK, I’m down to [do] it.’ ”</p><p>Only six teams will qualify for the Olympics, including the United States, which automatically has a spot as the host.</p><p>“In our house growing up, we’d always watch the Olympics,” Bregman said. “To have the opportunity to possibly play in the Olympics and represent our country would be like a dream come true.”</p><p>Crow-Armstrong was adamant that players should be empowered to make that decision individually. For himself, the answer wasn’t immediately clear.</p><p>“Obviously, I’d love to represent the U.S.,” he said. “But it would have to be logical. And it would have to work out with what we’re doing here [with the Cubs] and wherever the state of baseball is at that point.”</p><p>In the meantime, WBC results this year carry extra meaning. The WBC is the first of three Olympic qualifying tournaments. The two highest-placing WBC teams from the Americas that are not yet qualified will win spots in Los Angeles in 2028.</p>
entries.author: Maddie Lee

entries.title: Blackhawks allow another critical late goal, lose to Jets in overtime
entries.summary: <p>WINNIPEG, Manitoba — The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> have struggled to safely close out games this season. And in agonizing fashion, another lead slipped away Tuesday night in a 3-2 overtime loss to the Jets, who tied the score with 39 seconds left in regulation to spoil a strong 50-minute (but not 60-minute) effort.</p><p>“We’ve definitely been in a lot of tight games, which is a good thing in some ways,” Hawks coach <b>Jeff Blashill </b>said. “Sometimes it’s [about] finding that next goal. If you find a way to score the third one, the game is probably over. And if [the opponents] happen to get one [during] six-on-five [play], it’s not as big a deal.”</p><p>The Hawks have now allowed nine critical goals in the final four minutes of the third period this season, costing them at least eight points in the standings — basically the difference between a playoff-bubble team and a basement team.</p><p>Four of those goals were game-winners in regulation (Oct. 11 vs. Canadiens, Nov. 20 vs. Kraken, Dec. 16 at Maple Leafs and last Thursday at Predators). Five were tying goals by opponents that forced overtime (Oct. 19 vs. Ducks, Nov. 12 vs. Devils, Dec.  2 at Golden Knights, Jan. 27 at Wild and Tuesday at Jets). The Hawks ended up losing in four of those five overtimes.</p><p>Forward <b>Connor Bedard</b>, who continued his post-Olympics surge with two assists, said he has learned “little plays” can make enormous differences in three-on-three play.</p><p>“[Overtime is] man-on-man, so it’s hard to create too much on your own,” Bedard said. “[There are] turnovers here and there that lead to a chance, lead to a goal. Yeah, we’ve got to find a way to win more of them.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Jeff Blashill: "We played really good for lots of the game. We just stopped getting out of our zone clean. Because of that, we were in our end for the last 10-12 minutes. It's just a hard way to play. We didn't get out clean, and when we had it, I thought we had opportunities to…</p>— Ben Pope (@BenPopeCST) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCST/status/2029046518658023620?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 4, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p><h3>New responsibilities</h3><p>Any assumptions that Monday’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/02/blackhawks-trade-connor-murphy-oilers-stan-bowman-nhl-deadline" target="_blank" >trade of <b>Connor Murphy</b></a> would create more NHL opportunities for young defenseman <b>Kevin Korchinski </b>down the stretch look wrong already. With <b>Sam Rinzel</b> recovered from an illness and <b>Ethan Del Mastro</b> called up, Korchinski moved from in the lineup Sunday to out of the lineup Tuesday.</p><p>On the penalty kill, <b>Louis Crevier</b> and <b>Artyom Levshunov </b>will take on more responsibilities as right-handed defensemen in Murphy’s stead.</p><p>In terms of leadership, Bedard inherited the alternate-captain role vacated by Murphy and will wear an “A” for the rest of the season, Blashill confirmed. He’s probably the favorite to take over the captaincy next season if <b>Nick Foligno</b> retires.</p><p>“Obviously the letter doesn’t change anything you do as a person, but it’s nice to be recognized like that,” Bedard said.</p><h3>‘Not easy’ for Toews</h3><p>The good news for Hawks icon and Jets veteran <b>Jonathan Toews</b> is that he has stayed healthy enough to play in every game this season — a remarkable accomplishment at age 37 with his background.</p><p>The bad news is the Jets have greatly underperformed as a team, and Toews hasn’t been able to help them much.</p><p>Since his emotional Jan. 19 return to Chicago, the former Hawks captain has just one point (an assist) in 13 games. On the season, he has 19 points in 60 games.</p><p>“At this point of the season, I would love to have some more numbers to show for it and be able to feel like I contributed a little bit more offensively,” Toews said. “With that being said, it hasn’t been easy. [I’m] just working really hard on off-days to recover and get ready for the next one.”</p><p>“You’re not always going to be flying out there with the way the schedule is, but you’ve got to find a way to get your body and mind up for every single game. And I’ve been able to do that.”</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Masters of the rebuild, defending champion Thunder beat Bulls
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" target="_blank" >Bulls</a> are in the early stages of another rebuild. They’ve said goodbye to Nikola Vucevic, Coby White, Ayo Dosunmu and Kevin Huerter and hope building blocks <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/01/josh-giddey-gets-big-minutes-rewards-bulls-with-triple-double" target="_blank" >Josh Giddey</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2026/03/02/no-red-flag-bulls-matas-buzelis-forward-starting-surge" target="_blank" >Matas Buzelis</a> — with help from future draft picks — will move them closer to a championship.</p><p>On Tuesday, the Bulls faced a team that executed one of the best rebuilds in recent memory, losing to the defending champion Thunder 116-108 before a crowd of 18,561 at the United Center.</p><p>Buzelis, who had 11 points and five rebounds, rolled his right ankle and didn’t play after leaving with 19.2 seconds left in the third quarter. Giddey also rolled an ankle late in the third but played in the fourth, finishing with 14 points, 10 assists and nine rebounds.</p><p>Coach Billy Donovan said both suffered sprains and are day-to-day.</p><p>‘‘[On Wednesday], we’ll have a much better feel on both of them,’’ Donovan said.</p><p>Buzelis, who has played in every game this season, said he thinks he’ll be ready for the game Thursday in Phoenix.</p><p>‘‘I want to play every game,’’ Buzelis said. ‘‘We’ll see how it feels and go from there.’’</p><p>The Thunder already have gone pretty far down the road the Bulls are hoping to travel. They planted a seed for their project in 2019 by acquiring future MVP Shai Gilgeous-Alexander and a handful of first-round picks as part of a trade that sent Paul George to the Clippers. They subsequently drafted Chet Holmgren and Jalen Williams in 2022, assembling the core of a championship team that only needed time to mature and a few well-planned acquisitions to complete the cast.</p><p>Following that blueprint won’t be easy for the Bulls — or anybody else.</p><p>The Thunder were lucky Kawhi Leonard wanted the Clippers to acquire George. That stripped the Clippers of any leverage, something Thunder executive Sam Presti exploited beautifully. The Thunder were fortunate that Gilgeous-Alexander (who missed the game with an abdominal strain) grew from an 11th overall pick to one of the best players on the planet, though he was aided by the team’s development infrastructure.</p><p>‘‘Every situation’s different, so I don’t know if anything’s replicable,’’ Thunder coach Mark Daigneault said. ‘‘Situations change based on a million different things, a million different factors. Our success is a lot of work and certainly a lot of work by our players. But we’ve had a lot of luck, and I’m very careful because of that to come up here and give any sort of advice or speak from a level of standing that I don’t think is appropriate.</p><p>‘‘Their situation is different than ours, just like everybody. You need good fortune in different ways, and we’ve had it.’’</p><p>Donovan echoed Daigneault’s words, praising the Thunders drafting and development.</p><p>‘‘It probably went a lot faster than they thought,’’ Donovan said.</p><p>That’s what the Bulls would like to experience. They don’t want to become a team such as the Jazz or Wizards, franchises that have been stuck in perpetual rebuilds.</p><p>‘‘We’re in a spot where we’re trying to build out,’’ Donovan said. ‘‘A lot of it comes down to [having] good fortune. You want to develop the players that are already inside your organization. You want guys to be able to continue to grow. You want the chemistry on your team to be really, really good. You want guys to have the kind of mentality and the competitive makeup that you want to have and who you want to be as a team.’’</p>
entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: Dear Abby: Man has always enjoyed smooth silk on his skin
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> Since I was a small boy, silk fabric has always made me feel "safe." I remember wearing tights in the bathroom in front of the mirror or under my pajamas. Throughout the years, if nobody was around after work, I continued, but not around my wife, kids or now grandkids. I don't know why I enjoy them now in my 50s. Is this OK, or is something wrong with me? Am I missing one can in my six-pack? — <i>SMOOTH AS SILK IN VIRGINIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR SMOOTH:</b> I don't think you are missing anything in your six-pack or anywhere else. Males have been known to wear silk tights because it helps them stay warmer in cold weather. They have also been known to do it because it feels good next to their skin.</p><p>I wish you had mentioned why you felt it was necessary to smuggle this past your wife all these years, because there is nothing shameful about it. (Perhaps if you discuss it with her, she will tell you she wasn't fooled but never mentioned it because you didn't seem eager to talk.)</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> Yesterday, my wife and I went out to the cemetery to lay some flowers for her brother and father, who passed away many years ago. After we were finished and on our way out, I stopped for a few minutes to check on my first wife's crypt before returning to the car. When she asked what I had been doing, I told her I was making sure the plastic flowers were still there. My wife was surprised that I still check on her crypt because she had been gone for more than 16 years.</p><p>I married my second and current wife 15 years ago. It was a wonderful marriage — until now. She said her feelings were hurt that I was still checking out the crypt. She asked me how often I do it, and I told her twice a year. She's now upset with me. Was I wrong to pay my respects? My parents' crypts are nearby, and I check on theirs as well. — <i>STILL CARE IN THE WEST</i></p><p><b>DEAR STILL CARE:</b> Your wife is being childish, and I hope you will point that out to her. Much as she might wish otherwise, you came to her with a history. (You were, I assume, happily married before your first wife's death.) Tell "Number Two" that checking on your deceased wife's crypt isn't a threat to her unless she chooses to make it so, and that Dear Abby suggests she knock it off before she damages a good thing.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My wife threatens to divorce me in most every situation in which I drink alcohol. The context doesn't matter. Should I divorce her or try to work out another solution? — <i>THREATENED IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR THREATENED:</b> The first thing to do is understand why your wife feels as strongly as she does about your drinking. Does she have a family history in which alcohol played a role? Does your personality change when you drink socially? How much are you drinking on a daily basis? Are other relationships affected by your drinking? Once you have the answer to these questions, you can decide which is more important to you — the drinking or the marriage.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To receive a collection of Abby's most memorable — and most frequently requested — poems and essays, send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby — Keepers Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Trump's preemptive war with Iran could leave him boxed in
entries.summary: <p>As the joint military operation by the U.S. and Israel unfolds, officials in the Trump administration have taken pains to insist that this will not be “another Iraq.” The secretary of Defense, the vice president and the president himself have all publicly rejected comparisons to the 2003 invasion. There will be no occupation. No nation-building. No long-term ground commitment.</p><p>I suspect they mean it.</p><p>And yet, despite those assurances, a president who has spent years condemning the Iraq War as reckless and stupid may find himself in much the same place as his predecessor. The operation against Iran reveals the dangerous, enduring appeal of preemptive war for U.S. presidents.</p><p>There is no doubt that the Trump administration is pursuing a different tactical version of preemption than George W. Bush did. But the underlying reasoning is strikingly similar: Eliminate a future threat now, reshape the strategic landscape and avoid waiting for an imminent attack. </p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ff0000" name="module-ff0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>That logic is seductive. It promises decisiveness. It promises control over uncertainty. It suggests that force used today can prevent greater danger tomorrow.</p><p>That logic has always been powerful. It is also historically fraught.</p><p>It is now clear that this operation is targeting the regime itself, evidenced by the targeted killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. That’s no longer just about centrifuges. That’s regime decapitation territory.</p><p>The president’s language expanded to include freedom and democracy when he told The <a class="Link" href="https://www.washingtonpost.com/national-security/2026/02/28/trump-iran-war-regime-change-freedom/" target="_blank" >Washington Post</a> early Saturday morning: “All I want is freedom for the people.”</p><p>That is an ambitious goal — and it echoes 2003.</p><p>There are important differences in how the Trump administration is pursuing regime change. Most notably, it appears Trump is attempting to bring about regime change without boots on the ground. Instead, he has called on the Iranian people to take back their government, <a class="Link" href="https://china.usembassy-china.org.cn/president-donald-j-trump-on-the-united-states-military-combat-operations-in-iran/" target="_blank" >stating</a>, “When we are finished, take over your government. It will be yours to take. This will probably be your only chance for generations.”</p><h3>Striking only from the sky</h3><h3></h3><p>The interpretation seems straightforward: The U.S. will strike from the skies, but it will be up to the Iranian people to finish the job on the ground. History offers reason for caution. Regime change is rarely achieved through bombing campaigns alone.</p><p>The University of Chicago's <a class="Link" href="https://political-science.uchicago.edu/directory/Robert-Pape" target="_blank" >Robert Pape</a>, a leading expert on air power, offered a <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/ProfessorPape/status/2027756479785087326?s=20" target="_blank" >pointed reminder</a> the morning of the attack: “Air power rarely produces friendly regime change. Since WWI, dozens of bombing campaigns have tried to coerce governments from the air. None installed leaders more cooperative with the attacker.”</p><p>Air power can destroy things. It is far less reliable at constructing new political orders.</p><p>Preventive war is seductive for a reason. It promises the chance to eliminate threats before they mature. It allows leaders to seize the initiative rather than react to events. But it also creates escalation dynamics that are difficult to manage.</p><p>Iran is arguably the weakest it has been in decades. But weakened does not mean harmless. Iran retains the ability to retaliate. And unlike previous incidents, when Tehran carefully calibrated its response to avoid escalation, that restraint may not hold if the regime believes its survival is directly at stake. Iran’s retaliation will likely aim to impose costs — militarily and politically — in ways that pressure the administration to widen the conflict.</p><p>That is how limited wars become less limited.</p><p>A consequential difference between the Trump and Bush approaches is how little explanation we’ve received for the operation in Iran. The Bush administration made a concerted effort to sell the war to the American people, to Congress, and to the international community. We can spend plenty of time dissecting the flaws in that case, but at minimum, they made one.</p><p>Days into the operation, it is still not entirely clear what the goals are. This matters because goals dictate strategy. A campaign designed to degrade nuclear facilities looks very different from one designed to topple a regime and free the Iranian people. Without clarity of purpose, it is difficult to define success. In war, if you haven’t defined the finish line, it is hard to reach it.</p><p>The administration clearly wants to avoid “another Iraq.” But it is undertaking a risky operation that could leave it in a position not unlike the one George W. Bush ultimately faced. It is difficult to predict how this will unfold. But the possibility of being drawn into a wider, longer and more costly conflict is real.</p><p>Presidents can decide when a war starts. They rarely control how it concludes.</p><p><i>William Muck is a professor and chair of political science at North Central College in Naperville, where he teaches international politics and U.S. foreign policy. His research focuses on international security and military intervention.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-6a0000" name="module-6a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: William Muck

entries.title: In 'The Other Witch,' a Chicago dancer weaves a spell for women everywhere
entries.summary: <p>The chanting starts slowly, then builds as an eerie swirl of English, German and Turkish words fills the dark room. A shadowy figure rises beneath a heavy black cloak.</p><p>That shadow is Chicago dancer and choreographer Nejla Yatkin, whose slender frame seems to stretch beyond human scale as she begins her dance “<a class="Link" href="https://epiphanychi.com/events/the-other-witch/" target="_blank" ><u>The Other Witch</u></a>.” The work, which she will bring to Epiphany Center for the Arts this weekend, feels part sacred ritual, part scary movie.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div id="Form-newsletter-wrapper" class="Form-newsletter-wrapper" data-module data-has-background data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Yatkin’s solo piece is inspired by the work of 20th century German dance pioneer Mary Wigman and her 1914 work “Hexentanz,” or witch dance.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Mikaël Pasco</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As she prepares to revive the piece on International Women’s Day, in a former church, she said she feels a deep empowerment.</p><p>“I almost feel like I'm not dancing, I'm stepping aside, and something else is dancing through me,” said Yatkin, who performs the entire piece in a series of masks that block out almost all light. “That's an interesting but welcoming experience.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ff0000" name="module-ff0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>“The Other Witch”</h3><b>Where: </b>Epiphany Center For The Arts, 201 S. Ashland Ave.<br><b>When: </b>7:30 p.m., March 8<br><b>Info: </b><a class="Link" href="https://epiphanychi.com/events/the-other-witch/" target="_blank" ><u>Tickets from $30</u></a></div> </div><p>Yatkin began the project in earnest in 2018. With support from a Chicago Dancemakers Forum fellowship, she debuted a film version during the COVID-19 pandemic. But in some ways, the idea had been brewing since she was a teenager growing up in Berlin. That’s when Yatkin was first exposed to the German Expressionist Mary Wigman and the dance pioneer’s 1914 work “Hexentanz,” or witch dance.</p><p>The force of the dance shook a young Yatkin. “It always kind of scared me a bit,” Yatkin said. “Because it was so confrontational and forceful and I'm someone who is indirect.” Now, Yatkin is taking a cue from Wigman and embodying that spirit of witchy forcefulness. She never quotes from Wigman’s choreography directly, but early in her show, Yatkin projects a surviving clip of Wigman performing “Hexentanz.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-230000" name="image-230000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Yatkin’s witch dance is an ode to the enduring strength of womankind. As she prepares to perform it on International Women’s Day, she said she feels a deep sense of empowerment.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Enki Andrews</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“The piece is about reclaiming the power of women and sovereignty,” said Yatkin, 55. “The structures around you, of course, will try to diminish your power, but it is still up to you to express yourself in this world.”</p><p>Yatkin is the rare dance soloist staging full-length works in Chicago. After moving to the U.S. in the mid-’90s, Yatkin danced with companies in places like Denver and Dayton, Ohio, before going out on her own and eventually landing in Chicago in 2010. Her original works have earned her a prestigious Princess Grace Award and a Guggenheim Fellowship.</p><p>Scott Lundius, the executive director of the Morrison-Shearer Foundation, has known Yatkin for decades. The foundation — which is named, in part, after pioneering Chicago dancer Sybil Shearer — has also supported Yatkin’s work.</p><p>“She's one of those artists that when she's on stage with others, you can't look at anyone else,” Lundius said. “It's a rare thing when you're on stage and you have released yourself to the work, and you become a channel for the audience's experience of themselves. Nejla is a dancer who has the capacity to occupy that place for the audience.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fd0000" name="image-fd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/112c438/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa3%2F65%2F296eb4cc4f6196639b27dfb8835b%2Fwitchdance-260304-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e25467c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa3%2F65%2F296eb4cc4f6196639b27dfb8835b%2Fwitchdance-260304-2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7e52aa7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa3%2F65%2F296eb4cc4f6196639b27dfb8835b%2Fwitchdance-260304-2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/93f5a80/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa3%2F65%2F296eb4cc4f6196639b27dfb8835b%2Fwitchdance-260304-2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago dancer and choreographer Nejla Yatkin will perform her work “The Other Witch” at the Epiphany Center for the Arts this weekend. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Susan Manning, a scholar of dance history at Northwestern University, served as the dramaturg for “The Other Witch.” She said Yatkin’s captivating stage presence puts her in rare company with other vanguard soloists. “Wigman had that, Martha Graham had that. Nejla really has that quality.”</p><p>On a recent afternoon at Constellation on Western Avenue, Yatkin was wearing black sweats and puffy moon boots as she warmed up before a dress rehearsal. After her show at Chicago’s Epiphany, she will take “The Other Witch” on an international tour, with stops in Panama and Mexico.</p><p>Despite her dancing from a young age, Yatkin’s parents did not approve. To them, Yatkin said, dance was akin to prostitution. “I ran away with the circus,” she said. “I left home early before finishing high school to pursue my dream of dance.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/03/02/tiler-peck-dance-chicago-new-york-city-ballet-choreography" >Tiler Peck, America’s reigning star ballerina, brings her ‘love letter to dance’ to Chicago this week</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/dance/2026/03/03/joffrey-ballet-christopher-wheeldon-sleeping-beauty-john-neumeier-liliom-2026-2027-season" >Joffrey Ballet to be first American company to premiere Wheeldon’s ‘Sleeping Beauty’ and Neumeier’s ‘Liliom’ in 2026-2027</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/02/19/chicago-diversity-survey-arts-organizations-joffrey-ballet-goodman-theatre-architecture-center" >More of Chicago’s top arts organizations agree to share diversity data following WBEZ/Sun-Times survey</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>In “The Other Witch,” Yatkin said she is dancing for women who were also told “no.” She teared up as she recounted post-show interactions the work has prompted.</p><p>“Every time I perform, there's always a woman that comes to me and says, ‘I wanted to be a dancer, but my parents did not allow me to,’ and I was like, you can still dance,” said Yatkin. “Put the music on. Dance in your kitchen.”</p><p>She then disappeared backstage, reentering in a corset, floor-length black tutu and an intricately beaded mask that leaves her almost entirely blind to her surroundings.</p><p>“It really focuses your senses, because you don't have your eyes,” she said. “Your feet are more awake. Your hands are more awake. It shifts the whole way you are sensing the world. I almost feel like I'm in a cave dancing.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-170000" name="image-170000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a9f5daa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3321x1864+0+175/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F55%2F38a00ee7433e9dfe58bb1fffff66%2F300ppi-mg-0687.jpg"
