entries.author: Michael O'Brien

entries.title: Coalition appealing to Pope Leo to save St. Hubert School in Hoffman Estates from closing
entries.summary: <p>A group of parents at St. Hubert Catholic School in Hoffman Estates are praying a native Chicagoan will understand what it means to be on a mission from God to save a beloved church-run institution.</p><p>The parents are asking Pope Leo XIV — as well as their local pastor — to reconsider closing the 65-year-old school.</p><p>The Archdiocese of Chicago formally waived its own direct intervention this week.</p><p>A 30-day period known under Catholic canon law as a remonstratio ended Monday, which came at the request of St. Hubert supporters for Chicago Archdiocese Cardinal Blase Cupich to reconsider closure.</p><p>Archdiocese officials said they received the parents’ request and rejected it.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260303/education/coalition-appealing-to-pope-leo-to-save-st-hubert-school-in-hoffman-estates-from-closing/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com. </a><br></p>
entries.author: Daily Herald

entries.title: Bulls guard Josh Giddey hoping for an OKC-like turnaround for franchise
entries.summary: <p>Every team has a tell this time of year.</p><p>For Milwaukee, they have brought Giannis Antetokounmpo back despite heading for a play-in game at best.</p><p>Meanwhile, Memphis is in no hurry to bring Ja Morant back from an elbow injury, while Dallas is in no rush to bring back Cooper Flagg – and rightfully so – as the Rookie of the Year candidate has been dealing with a left midfoot sprain.</p><p>Then there’s the Bulls.</p><p>A roster left small and underwhelming in the frontcourt after the Feb. 5 trade deadline, but one that also has enough talent everywhere else to easily go 7-13 over the final 20 games. That’s why sitting in the No. 9 spot of the draft order pre-lottery feels nestled rather than turbulent.</p><p>The Bulls have the 10<sup>th</sup> easiest schedule remaining, have veteran free agents looking to make a statement for their next pay day, and have the heavy foot of coach Billy Donovan that knows pedal to the floor all the time.</p><p>The Grizzlies (sitting No. 8 in the lottery) have the fifth toughest schedule remaining, while the Mavericks (No. 7 in the lottery) have the fourth toughest.</p><p>As far as the six teams patrolling the bottom of the standings, too far for the Bulls to sink at this point, and much more committed to the process. The Bulls didn’t pick a direction until a month ago. Teams like Indiana, Washington, Brooklyn, Utah and Sacramento saw the writing on the wall by November.</p><p>Those teams also don’t have a Josh Giddey. Yes, the Bulls guard left the game against the Thunder in the second half with a tweaked ankle but afterward sounded like it was more bump in the road than setback as the Bulls venture out for a five-game West Coast trip starting on Thursday.</p><p>Giddey saw what a quick turnaround for a team looks like while playing in Oklahoma City and is hellbent on his current organization attempting to duplicate that. It’s almost like he’s willing to dip his toe in the lottery process this season because of where the roster is, and then it’s time to get back to climbing in the Eastern Conference.</p><p>“When I was in Oklahoma, we went from the worst team in the league to the best team in the league in 24 months,” Giddey said. “We did it pretty quickly. A lot of rebuilds don’t necessarily happen that fast. Obviously, with the people and personnel we had it got speeded up pretty quickly. Over those three years, we built a lot of really good habits, winning habits.”</p><p>Basically, what Donovan has been preaching throughout this disappointing season and will continue to preach, and while duplicating what the Thunder did is completely unrealistic, there’s no reason why the Bulls stay frequent guests in the lottery.</p><p>Giddey, Matas Buzelis, a hopefully healthy Jaden Ivey, money to spend on the likes of a restricted free agent like a Mark Williams, Walker Kessler or Tari Eason, and then wherever they land in the 2026 draft, and suddenly the Bulls are at least back fighting for mediocrity with a higher ceiling.</p><p>A starting five of Giddey, Ivey, Buzelis at his natural spot of the three, Jalen Smith and Williams with youngsters Keaton Wagler and Noa Essengue developing off the bench? Not OKC good by any means, but at least a foothold.</p><p>“It’s the little things that help you get back to that point,” Giddey said. “It doesn't happen overnight. These things take time to build. I believe in the guys in this locker room and the coaches and the people in this building that we have the ability to get there. But you can't skip steps in this process and everyone has to be bought in 100 percent and I think when you do that and find an identity as a team you can get yourself back into the picture.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Vegas odds consider Bears the favorite to land Raiders star Maxx Crosby
entries.summary: <p>At least one Las Vegas oddsmaker lists the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> as the favorite to land Maxx Crosby, even considering them more likely to employ the star edge rusher this fall than the team that currently signs his paycheck. Momentum continues to move in the Bears’ direction, too.</p><p>On Tuesday morning, DraftKings Sportsbook listed the Bears as having +200 odds to end up with the disgruntled defensive end. That means that a $100 bet would win $200. By Wednesday morning, the Bears were -145 favorites, meaning a $145 bet would win $100.<br>The Raiders are +165 to keep Crosby. The Cowboys (+800), Bills (+1200), Buccaneers and Patriots (each +1300) are among the other teams atop DraftKings’ list.</p><p>Crosby has been upset with the only team he’s ever known since the Raiders shut him down toward the end of last year while they (successfully) chased the league’s worst record to land the first pick in the draft. Raiders general manager John Spytek said last week the team expects Crosby to stay a Raider, but there’s no question teams have been inquiring about the Raiders’ asking price.</p><p>Crosby, who has a podcast and is outspoken on social media, hasn’t weighed in on his future. If he’s traded, it seems the Raiders will never have more suitors than they do now. The league year starts in one week, with free agent negotiations opening Monday.</p><p>Maxx Crosby and Bears quarterback Caleb Williams share a mutual respect — and spoke about their fondness for each other on a recent edition of Crosby’s podcast. The Bears, though, are facing a salary cap crunch and, after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/03/bears-drew-dalman-expected-to-retire-center-ryan-poles-falcons-stanford-nfl" target="_blank" >Drew Dalman’s retirement Tuesday</a>, have to find a new starting center.</p><p>Last week, general manager Ryan Poles pointed to another Bears-Raiders swap — the 2018 Khalil Mack trade — as a cautionary tale. The Bears pushed their chips in for Mack, trading two first-round picks — roughly the same price Crosby could fetch. They made the playoffs in their first year with Mack but never won a postseason game with him.</p><p>"You all have seen that, before I got here,” Poles said. “You can accelerate it and then after, it goes away pretty quick."<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: MLB suspends Braves' Jurickson Profar for season after second positive drug test
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — Atlanta Braves outfielder Jurickson Profar was suspended for the 2026 season by Major League Baseball on Tuesday following his second positive test for a performance-enhancing drug.</p><p>Profar tested positive for exogenous testosterone and its metabolites, the commissioner's office said, which means testosterone that was not produced by his body. Because it was a second offense, the length of Profar's suspension was 162 games.</p><p>The players' association filed a grievance at Profar's request to appeal to baseball's independent arbitrator, Martin F. Scheinman, a person familiar with the process told The Associated Press. The person spoke on condition of anonymity, because no announcement had been made.</p><p>An All-Star in 2024, Profar was suspended for 80 games last March 31 following a positive test for Chorionic Gonadotrophin (hCG), a hormone that helps production of testosterone. He issued a statement then saying: "I would never willingly take a banned substance, but I take full responsibility and accept MLB's decision."</p><p>His agent, Dan Lozano, did not immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>The Braves released a statement that they "were incredibly disappointed" about the failed drug test.</p><p>"Our players are consistently educated about the (MLB Drug Prevention and Treatment) Program and the consequences if they are found to be in violation," the club said. "The Atlanta Braves fully support the Program."</p><p>Profar homered in his return from suspension on July 2 and finished with a .245 average, 14 homers, 43 RBIs and a .787 OPS in 80 games. He batted .280 in 2024, when he set career highs with 24 homers, 85 RBIs and an .839 OPS.</p><p>Profar said at the start of spring training that he had sports hernia surgery in November, requiring a six-week recovery time. He has appeared in four spring training games this year, going 3 for 10 with three RBIs.</p><p>Under the suspension, he is ineligible for the postseason and the World Baseball Classic. A native of Curaçao, Profar had been set to play for the Netherlands.</p><p>Profar will lose his $15 million salary for this year as part of a $42 million, three-year contract through 2027. He lost half his $12 million salary in 2025 due to the initial suspension.</p><p>He became the seventh player suspended 162 games for a second PED infraction after New York Mets pitcher Jenrry Mejia (July 2015), Cleveland outfielder Marlon Byrd (June 2016), free agent catcher Cody Stanley (July 2016), Houston pitcher Francis Martes (February 2020), Mets second baseman Robinson Canó (November 2020) and Milwaukee pitcher J.C. Mejia (September 2023).</p><p>Mejia received a lifetime ban in February 2016 after a third positive test, the only player to be given a permanent ban since drug testing with penalties started in 2004.</p><p>Four players had been suspended previously this year for positive tests, including free agent outfielder Max Kepler for 80 games under the major league program following a positive test for Epitrenbolone.</p><p>Following the offseason signing of left fielder Mike Yastrzemski to a $23 million, two-year deal, Profar had been targeted to be the Braves' primary designated hitter.</p><p>When catcher Sean Murphy returns from a hip injury, perhaps in May, 2025 NL Rookie of the Year Drake Baldwin could fill in at DH when not behind the plate.</p><p>With Yastrzemski, Michael Harris and Ronald Acuña Jr. in the outfield, Eli White could be a DH option. The Braves also are without projected starting shortstop Ha-seong Kim due to a finger injury. Mauricio Dubon, expected to serve a utility role, is scheduled to open the season as the starting shortstop.</p><p>The loss of Profar could create an opportunity for Dominic Smith, who signed a minor league deal on Feb. 17.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: El Chicago Fire comienza la construcción de un estadio de $750 millones en el South Loop
entries.summary: <p>El Chicago Fire inició el martes la construcción de un estadio de fútbol por $750 millones en el South Loop, lo que también representa un primer paso para el desarrollo de la parcela inactiva conocida como The 78.</p><p>El estadio, con capacidad para 22,000 espectadores y financiado por Joe Mansueto, el propietario multimillonario del Fire, se espera que esté listo a tiempo para la temporada 2028-29 del equipo. El estadio de fútbol será un gran impulso para el equipo, que actualmente juega en Soldier Field, pero ha tenido que trasladar sus partidos a los suburbios debido a conflictos de programación con los Chicago Bears y conciertos.</p><p>“Esta es la culminación de un trabajo que busca transformar algunas de las piezas de terreno más valiosas y prominentes en la ciudad de Chicago en un estadio de clase mundial y una comunidad vibrante en su alrededor”, dijo la concejal Pat Dowell (3°), cuyo distrito incluye el proyecto.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-4d0000" name="module-4d0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El estadio servirá como un ancla para The 78, el sitio de 62 acres en Roosevelt Road y Clark Street que ha eludido el desarrollo durante años. El terreno, propiedad de Related Midwest, es una de las parcelas no desarrolladas más grandes que quedan en la ciudad.</p><p>La primera fase de la construcción en The 78 incluirá más de 1,400 pies de espacio frente al río accesible al público, una nueva parada de taxi acuático, 1.5 millas de senderos para bicicletas, estaciones de bicicletas Divvy, dos lotes de estacionamiento temporal, estacionamiento público y 3 acres de campos deportivos, según Related Midwest.</p><p>Una vez que el proyecto de 62 acres esté completo, se estima que generará $8 mil millones en impacto económico. El desarrollo también incluirá vivienda, senderos y una conexión al parque Ping Tom Memorial Park.</p><p>“Esto no sólo será un gran lugar para venir a ver un partido de fútbol o un concierto, será un lugar fantástico para llamar hogar para miles de habitantes de Chicago”, dijo Dave Baldwin, presidente de operaciones comerciales del Chicago Fire.</p><p>Mansueto tuvo dos opciones al seleccionar el hogar permanente del equipo: The 78 y el ahora desaparecido megadesarrollo de Lincoln Yards. Lincoln Yards colapsó el año pasado y una gran parte del proyecto en el lado norte fue adquirida por JDL Development, con sede en Chicago, que recibió la aprobación del Concejo Municipal el mes pasado para una visión reducida del sitio.</p><p>Mansueto dijo que “se enamoró de inmediato” del 78 y sus vistas pintorescas de Chicago. Y un estadio de fútbol ya era un uso permitido en el sitio.</p><p>“Sabía que era el hogar adecuado para el Fire”, agregó. “Es realmente perfecto. Creo que el modelo correcto hoy para construir un vecindario es tener un estadio como ancla. Así que estamos muy orgullosos de ser el ancla del sitio de The 78 y realmente hacer que este sitio avance”.</p><p>El terreno, una vez propiedad del operador político convicto Tony Rezko, había sido considerado para ser la segunda sede mundial de Amazon. También estaba en carrera para convertirse en la nueva sede corporativa de United Airlines, el nuevo hogar de JPMorgan Chase y el lugar de un casino.</p><p>La Universidad de Illinois en 2024 se retiró de los planes para construir su centro de tecnología e investigación de $300 millones, Discovery Partners Institute, eligiendo en su lugar el parque cuántico industrial, Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park, y comprando un edificio en el Loop.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/06/03/chicago-fire-planea-un-estadio-de-futbol-en-area-del-the-78-en-south-loop" target="_blank" >Los bocetos, publicados en junio, muestran un estadio</a> que se asemeja a un almacén, evocando las raíces de la fabricación industrial de Chicago, con los aficionados ingresando por Roosevelt Road.</p><p>El estadio y sus distritos circundantes están diseñados para ser un motor económico durante todo el año. Baldwin dijo que se llevarán a cabo eventos que van desde partidos de rugby y lacrosse hasta conciertos y eventos corporativos en el estadio.</p><p>“Esperamos que el estadio del Chicago Fire sea un ancla en The 78 que atraiga recreación, gastronomía, espacios culturales e inversiones para toda nuestra ciudad”, dijo el alcalde Brandon Johnson.</p><p>También realizó una ligera crítica a los Chicago Bears, que han provocado una <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/02/26/chicago-bears-property-tax-incentives-illinois-house-city-opposition-arlington-heights-proposed-stadium" target="_blank" >guerra de ofertas</a> entre Illinois e Indiana por la franquicia de la NFL.</p><p>“Diré que es muy bueno asegurarse de que al menos uno de nuestros equipos de fútbol en Chicago sea bueno”, dijo Johnson. “¡Queda uno pendiente!”</p><p>El estadio del Fire ganó momentum inmediato y una rápida aprobación porque Mansueto está financiando el proyecto. El desarrollo también ha recibido elogios de los residentes del South Loop, aunque algunos en Chinatown y otros vecindarios cercanos han expresado preocupaciones sobre el desarrollo que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/07/30/chinatown-chicago-fire-stadium" target="_blank" >desplaza a los residentes de mucho tiempo</a> y a negocios pequeños.</p><p>A medida que comienza la construcción del estadio, el futuro permanece incierto para los White Sox, cuyo presidente, Jerry Reinsdorf, alguna vez consideró al 78 como un lugar potencial para un nuevo estadio. El acuerdo de desarrollo del terreno no permite un segundo estadio, a menos que los desarrolladores regresen ante la Comisión de Planificación de Chicago y ante la comunidad para su consideración.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: El ataque de Irán no es más que una táctica para desviar la atención
entries.summary: <p>La seguridad nacional, sí, como no. El <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2026/02/28/iran-trump-israel" target="_blank" >ataque de Donald Trump a Irán</a> fue una audaz maniobra política, destinada a desviar y distraer de, bueno, de todo.</p><p>Incluso Pearl Harbor —el mayor ataque premeditado en suelo estadounidense antes del 11 de septiembre— no fue respondido antes de que el presidente Franklin Roosevelt se dirigiera al Congreso y solicitara una declaración formal de guerra contra Japón.</p><p>No se engañen; asesinar al líder de una potencia extranjera, sin importar cuán malvado sea, sin una provocación directa, es un acto de guerra.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-500000" name="module-500000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Con un Congreso sin columna vertebral, casi completamente complaciente, el corrupto balón avanza otras 10 yardas hacia la zona final del autoritarismo total.</p><p>Cada uno de nosotros —excepto el senador Lindsey Graham, republicano de Carolina del Sur, cuya exhibición de adulonería sin vergüenza en entrevista con "<a class="Link" href="https://www.nbcnews.com/meet-the-press/video/march-1-sen-lindsey-graham-sen-mark-kelly-and-rep-ro-khanna-258511429523" target="_blank" >Meet the Press</a>" haría que su propio perro se escapara de casa— debería estar temblando en sus botas. El tercer juicio político, seguido de la destitución, debería venir rápido y contundente. Pero, por supuesto, no pasará.</p><p><i>Rob Hirsh, West Ridge </i></p><h3>Detengan al ‘loco’</h3><p>El Sun-Times del sábado incluyó una <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2026/02/28/nx-s1-5730151/trump-iran-nuclear-talks" target="_blank" >noticia</a> de la Associated Press que citaba a Donald Trump diciendo de los iraníes, “No estoy contento con el hecho de que no están dispuestos a darnos lo que tenemos que tener... Veremos qué pasa. Hablaremos más tarde”.</p><p>Horas (o tal vez sólo minutos) después, surgieron informes de que Estados Unidos e Israel atacaron Irán.</p><p>Este llamado presidente nunca ha tenido que esperar por lo que quiere y no le importa que muchos estadounidenses, a quienes dice representar, no quieren más guerras de cambio de régimen. También, obviamente, no le importa la diplomacia y las negociaciones en el Medio Oriente. No merece ser recogedor de perros, mucho menos presidente.</p><p>Puede que sean las últimas acusaciones inventadas de Israel y Trump, o tal vez otro de esos sueños febrilmente amargos y mezquinos, pero ¿por qué nuevamente estamos sirviendo ciegamente a los caprichos de Benjamin Netanyahu, y por qué ningún republicano defiende a "Nosotros, el Pueblo", y detiene a este loco?</p><p><i>Maja Ramírez, Avondale </i></p><h3>Estados Unidos también necesita un cambio de régimen</h3><p>Donald Trump está instando a los iraníes a hacerse cargo de su país y promover el cambio de régimen. ¿Quién se cree que es? Ni siquiera puede manejar a su propio país. Pido a mis compatriotas estadounidenses a hacerse cargo y derrocar al régimen que dirige Estados Unidos. Hagamos un cambio de régimen aquí antes de que sea demasiado tarde. Deshagámonos del tirano.</p><p><i>Jackie Tinker, Des Plaines </i></p><h3>Barron Trump puede enlistarse</h3><p>Donald Trump habla con valentía sobre ir a la guerra con Irán. Si está verdaderamente confiado en esa decisión, entonces quizás las primeras botas en el terreno deberían pertenecer a su propio hijo, Barron Trump. Es mucho más fácil abogar por sacrificios y la inevitable pérdida de vidas cuando recae sobre las familias de otros.</p><p><i>Tom Scorby, St. Charles </i></p><h3>Guerra y paz</h3><p>A la atención de: Oslo, Noruega (donde se ubica el centro Nobel).</p><p>Hay una nueva lógica de MAGA: el camino hacia el Premio Nobel de la Paz es atacar otros países.</p><p><i>Hugh Spencer, Countryside </i></p><h3>¿Hombre de paz?</h3><p>¿No es interesante que Donald Trump, el hombre que ama presumir que <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/02/25/state-union-speech-trumps-lies-immigrants-america-chicago-natalie-moore" target="_blank" >terminó ocho guerras</a>, haya comenzado una guerra armada con Irán?</p><p><i>Steven Herr, West Ridge </i></p><h3>El ataque a Irán viola la Constitución de Estados Unidos</h3><p>El actual ataque a Irán no es solo un ataque a Irán. Es un ataque a la Constitución de Estados Unidos. El poder para declarar la guerra está otorgado al Congreso, no al presidente. Incluso después del bombardeo de Pearl Harbor, el presidente Franklin Roosevelt se dirigió al Congreso y solicitó una declaración formal de guerra.</p><p>Esta es la segunda vez que Donald Trump ha tomado acción militar contra Irán sin la autorización del Congreso. En el <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2025/06/21/u-s-has-struck-3-iranian-nuclear-sites-trump-says-joining-israeli-air-campaign" target="_blank" >bombardeo de junio</a>, Trump afirmó haber aniquilado el programa de armas nucleares iraní. La existencia continua de ese programa es ahora parte de la justificación para la guerra actual.</p><p>Trump, por supuesto, ya ha atacado la Constitución antes, cuando unilateralmente y sin autorización del Congreso buscó imponer aranceles. Después de que la Corte Suprema <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/02/20/supreme-court-trump-sweeping-tariffs-john-roberts-economic-agenda" target="_blank" >fallara</a> recientemente en su contra, <a class="Link" href="https://www.democracynow.org/2026/2/23/lori_wallach_trade_tariffs" target="_blank" >atacó</a> a la corte.</p><p>También atacó la Constitución al intentar distorsionar la <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2025/01/21/birthright-citizenship-donald-trump-executive-order-14th-amendment" target="_blank" >14ª Enmienda</a> en sus intentos de terminar con la ciudadanía de nacimiento.</p><p>Y luego hay otro ataque propuesto a la Constitución a medida que Trump busca que el gobierno federal asuma el poder de llevar a cabo elecciones, alejándolo de los gobiernos estatales.</p><p>Los votantes están legítimamente preocupados por la economía, pero incluso si la economía fuera fuerte, la acción esencial para asegurarse de que un "gobierno del pueblo, por el pueblo y para el pueblo" no perezca es defender la Constitución.</p><p><i>Alan Rhine, Glenview </i></p><h3>Trump siguió la ley</h3><p>Para quienes quieren criticar al presidente Donald Trump por bombardear Irán, háganlo, pero por favor no afirmen que él está violando de alguna manera la Resolución de Poderes de Guerra o que actúa “inconstitucionalmente”. No lo está.</p><p>La <a class="Link" href="https://www.congress.gov/bill/93rd-congress/house-joint-resolution/542" target="_blank" >resolución</a> fue aprobada por el Congreso en 1973 a pesar del veto del presidente Richard Nixon. Como cuestión preliminar, no está claro si la resolución en sí es constitucional. Los presidentes —tanto demócratas como republicanos— han argumentado que no lo es. La Corte Suprema nunca se ha pronunciado al respecto.</p><p>Así como el Congreso no puede aprobar una ley que prohíba la Primera Enmienda —ya que eso requeriría un proceso separado de enmienda de la Constitución— también se argumenta que el Congreso no puede infringir los poderes del presidente como comandante en jefe mediante una legislación.</p><p>Pero incluso si la Resolución de Poderes de Guerra es constitucional, Trump no la ha violado.</p><p>Todo lo que requiere es que él notifique al Congreso de sus acciones dentro de las 48 horas, lo cual hizo. Luego, tiene hasta 90 días para retirar tropas sin el consentimiento del Congreso. Si quiere mantener tropas en el terreno por más de 90 días, necesita que el Congreso lo autorice.</p><p>No parece que haya tropas en el terreno en Irán, pero incluso si las hubiera, lo más probable es que sean retiradas muy dentro de los 90 días. Si no, entonces y solo entonces, el Congreso necesitaría aprobar, suponiendo que la Resolución de Poderes de Guerra sea efectivamente constitucional.</p><p>Así que, de nuevo, felicita o critica al presidente cuanto quieras, sólo no afirmes que está violando la Constitución o la ley.</p><p><i>William Choslovsky, Sheffield Neighbors </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" >La Voz Chicago</a></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Girl, 11, allegedly assaulted outside Near South Side elementary school
entries.summary: <p>An 11-year-old girl was taken to a hospital after school Monday after three boys allegedly sexually assaulted her on the Near South Side, Chicago police said.</p><p>Chicago police said the girl was outside between 3:30 p.m. and 3:50 p.m. in the 2200 block of South Federal Street when three boys she knows approached and sexually assaulted her before fleeing. </p><p>Paramedics were called to 55 W. Cermak Road, the National Teachers Academy elementary school, and the girl was taken to Lurie Children's Hospital, officials confirmed, but no further details were shared. </p><p>A representative for the Chicago Public Schools released a statement from the school's principal, Teneka Brooks, which said it "received a report that members of our school community engaged improperly with another school community member outside of our school building shortly after dismissal today."</p><p>"This reported improper engagement did not involve any adults," according to the statement, sent to parents Monday. "Safety continues to be my top priority," the principal said in the statement. </p><p>"If your child is aware of this matter and has voiced any concerns to you, please let us know so we can ensure their social-emotional needs are met," the statement said. </p><p>The school notified police, the CPS Office of Safety and Security, and the CPS Office of Student Protections and Title IX, according to the statement. </p><p>Detectives are investigating. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Steppenwolf's next season to feature works by Adam Rapp, Branden Jacobs-Jenkins
entries.summary: <p>Chicago's Steppenwolf Theatre will stage two world premiere plays next season, including “Night Fawn” by the Tony-nominated and Pulitzer Prize finalist Adam Rapp.</p><p>Wednesday's announcement for the 2026-2027 season comes on the heels of the theater’s landmark 50th season featuring a show directed by Robert Falls and a premiere from “Moonlight” writer Tarell Alvin McCraney.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div id="Form-newsletter-wrapper" class="Form-newsletter-wrapper" data-module data-has-background ><a class="AnchorLink" check your inbox for your confirmation.</p> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-description">Stay ahead of what’s hitting Chicago stages, galleries, museums and more with Green Room, WBEZ’s weekly arts & culture newsletter!</div> <div class="Form-newsletter-errorMessage"><p>Sorry, there was an error registering your email.</p></div> </div> <form data-ajax-endpoint="https://sfmc-subscribe.wbez.org/v1/subscription" class="Form-newsletter-form" method="POST" > <div class="Form-newsletter-item"> <div class="EmailInput"><label type="submit">Sign Up </button> </div> <div class="Form-items-item"> <input type="hidden" name="acquisition_campaign" value="ARTS_Affordance"> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-disclaimer"> <p>By subscribing, you agree with WBEZ’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/terms-of-use" target="_blank" >Terms of Use</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/privacy-policy" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>With its next season, artistic directors Glenn Davis and Audrey Francis said Steppenwolf is eager to maintain its reputation as “the place” to see the next big thing in American theater.</p><p>“Steppenwolf has been known over the years for producing some pretty innovative work,” said Davis. “The last few years we’ve continued to send shows to other markets, whether Los Angeles or New York, that started in Chicago. We’re really proud of the audiences that we have here, but its also important that we stay in the national consciousness.”</p><p>Rapp's haunting “Night Fawn” will star ensemble member Cliff Chamberlain as a man who has lost his job, ended his marriage and is now heading home to rural Illinois to settle the affairs of his late mother.</p><p>“Reading that play, just reading it as an experience, I thought, ‘This is like a novel on stage,’” Francis said of the brooding script. “How lucky for us to get the opportunity to deconstruct what a play can actually be and what the written word can be.”</p><p>Francis herself will star in the second world premiere, “Adirondack Chair Circle,” by Chicago playwright Stephanie Alison Walker. The comedy will be directed by Tony Award-winner Pam MacKinnon. Francis said she’s excited to be working with “two powerhouse women.”</p><p>“What I love about the play so much is that Stephanie has written a really funny script that has a beautiful tone of satire and absurdity and also biting comedy,” said Francis. “But at the same time she is calling our moral identity into question. In true Steppenwolf fashion, we've programmed a comedy that is a catalyst for your moral compass to betray itself.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“The last few years we’ve continued to send shows to other markets, whether Los Angeles or New York, that started in Chicago,” Glenn Davis said. That includes the recent Broadway run of “Purpose,” the Tony-winning play written by Branden Jacobs-Jenkins. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Marc J Franklin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Davis will also take the stage in the 51st season. He will go toe-to-toe with Namir Smallwood, who is currently on Broadway in Steppenwolf’s “Bug” opposite <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/movies-tv/white-lotus-carrie-coon-steppenwolf-theatre" target="_blank" ><u>Carrie Coon</u></a>, in Suzan-Lori Parks’ Pulitzer Prize-winning drama “Topdog/Underdog.”</p><p>“It's like a cage match,” said Davis, who also had a recent run on Broadway with his role in “Purpose,” a performance that earned his first Tony nomination. “Two actors in there, and they're just like, ‘Go’. The language is so beautiful and so visceral, it feels like one of those plays that could have been born out of the Steppenwolf ethos and philosophy of pushing your partner and pushing the boundaries of what theater is capable of.”</p><p>The season will also feature the Chicago premiere of “The Comeuppance,” by Jacobs-Jenkins of “Purpose,” and the English language premiere of the psychological thriller “Ellen B.,” which will be helmed by Chicago native and Tony-nominated director Whitney White.</p><p>This marks the sixth season that Davis and Francis have led the theater's artistic team and the fourth season that they programmed. </p>
entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: Durante audiencia, Kristi Noem dijo no conocer a Marimar Martínez, la mujer de Chicago a la que un agente de ICE le disparó
entries.summary: <p>Marimar Martínez, la mujer de Chicago que sobrevivió a haber sido baleada cinco veces por agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza el año pasado, se encontraba a sólo cinco filas detrás de la secretaria del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS), Kristi Noem durante una tensa audiencia del Comité Judicial del Senado el martes mientras era presionada por el senador Richard Blumenthal para que reconociera que el tiroteo fue “incorrecto”.</p><p>Con Blumenthal describiendo el <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" >tiroteo de octubre que dejó a Martínez con daños</a> a los nervios de su mano derecha, el demócrata de Connecticut le preguntó a Noem si el tiroteo de Martínez fue “incorrecto” y también destacó —a través de grandes letreros— imágenes de mensajes de texto que mostraban al agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/11/agentes-dijeron-es-hora-de-ser-agresivos-en-video-que-contradice-las-afirmaciones-contra-marimar-martinez" target="_blank" >Charles Exum presumiendo del tiroteo</a> y alegando haber escrito en inglés: “Le disparé 5 balas y ella tenía 7 agujeros. Tomen nota, muchachos”.</p><p>A pesar de la amplia cobertura del tiroteo de Martínez y de las <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/11/21/desestiman-el-caso-contra-mujer-a-la-que-le-disparo-agente-de-la-patrulla-fronteriza-en-brighton-park" target="_blank" >acusaciones de obstruir la justicia que luego fueron desestimadas</a> en su contra, Noem afirmó repetidamente que no conocía el caso.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-aa0000" name="module-aa0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Señor, no conozco la situación ni el caso”, dijo Noem. “Lo investigaré para asegurarme de que todos los procedimientos se hayan seguido correctamente”.</p><p>Noem tampoco proporcionó detalles sobre si Exum, el agente que le disparó a Martínez, sigue en su puesto, “portando un arma”. Un portavoz de la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP) dijo el mes pasado al Chicago Sun-Times que a Exum lo habían descansado. El portavoz no respondió a un correo electrónico de seguimiento que preguntaba cuándo comenzó la licencia administrativa o si todavía está en efecto. Noem dijo que no conocía los detalles, pero que “averiguará y enviaré esa información”.</p><p>“Creo que a los estadounidenses les parecería absolutamente aterrador que ese agente siga en el trabajo, portando un arma”, dijo Blumenthal. “Y el hecho de que no puedes decirme que lo quitarás de su puesto”.</p><p>El testimonio de Noem en Washington ante el Congreso ocurre mientras el financiamiento de DHS permanece detenido durante un cierre parcial del gobierno, con el Congreso en un punto muerto por las políticas de inmigración. A pesar del cierre, los senadores se centraron principalmente en la aplicación de la ley del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) en las principales ciudades y los tiroteos fatales de Alex Pretti y Renee Good por agentes federales en Minnesota.</p><p>Martínez emitió un comunicado el martes diciendo que las afirmaciones de Noem sobre no conocer su caso vienen a ser “lo contrario a la evidencia que publicamos en mi caso”. Dijo que los mensajes de texto prueban que Exum contó con el apoyo de Noem después del tiroteo y quiere que Noem investigue a Exum “por su intento de ejecución”.</p><p>“Francamente, creo que es aún peor si la secretaria Noem no conoce los hechos de mi caso”, agregó Martínez. “Ella es la líder de DHS y uno de sus agentes intentó matarme, a una ciudadana estadounidense, en las calles de Chicago. Se informó en todos los medios nacionales durante meses. ¿No lee el periódico? ¿No le informan sobre los tiroteos de ciudadanos estadounidenses por agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza? Que la líder del DHS no sepa sobre el tiroteo de una ciudadana estadounidense, en nuestro propio país, por uno de sus agentes es más alarmante que la negativa de su agencia a investigar estas acciones ilegales”.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-14d75d" name="image-14d75d"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Mensajes de texto del agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza Charles Exum" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f92a41f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+515/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F34%2F74%2F0fdd6ad849ef8aee3251a30994c9%2Ftext.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8034a58/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+515/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F34%2F74%2F0fdd6ad849ef8aee3251a30994c9%2Ftext.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f92a41f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+515/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F34%2F74%2F0fdd6ad849ef8aee3251a30994c9%2Ftext.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mensajes de texto del agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza Charles Exum después del tiroteo el 4 de octubre de Marimar Martínez en Chicago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Fiscalía de los Estados Unidos</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El senador Dick Durbin también le preguntó a Noem sobre los asesinatos de Pretti y Good en Minneapolis, así como el tiroteo de Martínez en Chicago. En cada caso, DHS etiquetó a las víctimas como “terroristas domésticos”.</p><p>“Tenemos amplias pruebas en video y testimonios de testigos que demuestran que estás equivocada. Tus declaraciones causaron un dolor inconmensurable a estas familias”, le dijo Durbin a Noem. “Déjame darte la oportunidad de hacer lo correcto. ¿Retirarás estas declaraciones identificando a estos individuos como terroristas domésticos?”.</p><p>Noem calificó los tiroteos como “situaciones trágicas” y dijo que “siempre ofrecemos nuestras condolencias a esas familias”. La jefa del DHS afirmó que se estaba “basando en informes desde el terreno y de los agentes que están allí y trabajan para ser transparentes”.</p><p>Durbin le preguntó a Noem cómo el apodarles “terroristas domésticos en el lugar de los hechos iba a calmar la situación”.</p><p>“Estamos trabajando en esas situaciones donde hay una pérdida trágica de vida y en las que nuestros agentes están involucrados, de las que seguimos brindando información”, aseguró Noem.</p><p>“¿Es tan difícil decir que te equivocaste?”, interrumpió Durbin.</p><p>“... Me esfuerzo absolutamente por proporcionar información factual y continuaremos haciéndolo”, dijo Noem.</p><p>“Y cuando fallas, ¿lo admites públicamente?”, preguntó Durbin. Noem respondió, “Absolutamente. Siempre sabemos que hay espacio para mejorar”.</p><p>Noem también fue cuestionada repetidamente sobre el aliado de Trump y exasesor Steve Bannon, quien expresó su apoyo a la iniciativa de Trump de nacionalizar las elecciones, incluyendo el despliegue de funcionarios de ICE en los lugares de votación.</p><p>“No hay planes para tener oficiales de ICE en nuestros lugares de votación”, aseguró Noem.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" >La Voz Chicago</a></p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Chicago talents Kaicrewsade, Squirrel Flower and m.e.h. take center stage at Thalia Hall Free For All
entries.summary: <p>Thalia Hall’s biannual “building crawl” is back this weekend with a slew of local music and vendors.</p><p>At 4 p.m. on Saturday the building opens all its doors for a free building-wide party with programming on Thalia Hall’s mainstage, in first-floor piano bar Tack Room and basement bar Punch House.</p><p>The last venue’s “Free For All” was in August 2025. This year’s spring edition is headlined by Philadelphia band Feeble Little Horse and Chicago-based indie rock artist Squirrel Flower. WBEZ is a co-presenter.</p><p>Chicago talent also taking the stage are genre-bending musician <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2024/11/19/chicago-singer-m-e-h-on-how-to-cultivate-a-fearless-personal-style" target="_blank" ><u>m.e.h.</u></a> and rapper <a class="Link" href="https://vocalo.org/2025/07/25/kaicrewsade-chicago-artist-lollapalooza-debut" target="_blank" ><u>Kaicrewsade</u></a>, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2025/12/17/best-new-albums-2025-chicago-musicians-bands-artists" target="_blank" ><u>whose 2025 albums</u></a> were both favorites of Vocalo and WBEZ. More local music includes jazz-turned-experimental country trio Alta Vista, indie folk duo Sleeper’s Bell, piano man Kevan Eftekhari and indie solo artist Astrachan.</p><p>The party also includes bingo co-hosted by Marijuana Brothers and Vocalo’s Nudia Hernandez, plus the Muñeca Gigante Drag Show hosted by Cléo La Muñeca.</p><p><a class="Link" href="http://thaliahallchicago.com/freeforall" target="_blank" ><u>Admission is free with RSVP</u></a>, but organizers are accepting on-site donations to benefit Pilsen Food Pantry.</p>
entries.author: Morgan Ciocca | Vocalo

entries.title: Man charged in ambush shooting at cops in Auburn Gresham
entries.summary: <p>A 31-year-old man is scheduled to appear before a judge Wednesday, accused of creeping up behind several police officers and shooting at them during a prostitution investigation Monday morning in Auburn Gresham. </p><p>Sedrick Griffin, was charged with attempted first-degree murder, aggravated discharge of a firearm and possessing a firearm while being a repeat felon, among other charges, Chicago police said. </p><p>Officers were inside two marked squad cars just after 6 a.m. Monday, responding to a call of solicitation of prostitution in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/02/gunman-shoots-at-chicago-cops-leading-to-chase-crash-in-auburn-gresham" target="_blank" >7500 block of South Peoria Street</a> when Griffin arrived in a black Nissan Armada. He crept up behind a pickup truck before shooting at the officers, according to a police report obtained by the Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>Griffin, 31, then allegedly fled in the black SUV, and officers chased him until he crashed into two parked vehicles in the 7600 block of South Union Avenue, police said.</p><p>He tried running away but was arrested and taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center for observation, officials said.</p><p>Griffin, of the Garfield Park neighborhood, was scheduled to appear for a detention hearing before a Cook County judge at the Leighton Criminal Court building Wednesday.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Chicago man charged in theft of van filled with puppies in Lincoln Park
entries.summary: <p>A Chicago man was expected in court Wednesday, charged with stealing a purple van with at least eight puppies inside that belonged to a Lincoln Park pet salon. </p><p>There were no reports of injuries to the dogs. </p><p>Edwin Herrera, 46, was charged with several charges of theft and felony counts of possession of a stolen vehicle, possession of burglary tools and possessing a stolen or altered title and registration, Chicago police said. </p><p>Around 11:10 a.m. Tuesday, a purple 2021 Ford van that was reported stolen from Animal Lover's Pet Salon, 2232 N. Clybourn Ave., earlier in the day was seen traveling north on Clark Street with swapped license plates, according to police reports obtained by the Chicago Sun-Times. </p><p>Officers left their emergency lights off and followed the van until it stopped in an alley in the 1200 block of West Oakdale Avenue in the Lake View neighborhood where the puppies were seen in cages, police said. </p><p>Herrera was seen walking out of a garage and was arrested after he matched the description of the van driver based on surveillance captured near where the van was stolen, officials said. </p><p>A flathead screwdriver was found at the scene and matched flathead screws that were used to change the license plates, according to the report. </p><p>Herrera, of the Austin neighborhood, was set to appear before a Cook County judge at the Leighton Criminal Courthouse Wednesday. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 18 things to do in Chicago: March 5-11
entries.summary: <h3>Theater</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-fb638d83-15b7-11f1-859f-4fbd3d83f61e"><li>The Goodman Theatre presents <b>“Theater of the Mind,”</b> David Byrne and Mala Gaonkar’s 75-minute journey of self-reflection, discovery and imagination, inspired by and grounded in neuroscience. Guides take 16 audience members at a time through a 15,000-square-foot intimate and immersive installation. Andrew Scoville directs. From March 11-May 31 at Reid Murdoch Building, 333 N. LaSalle Drive. Tickets: $66-$91. Visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.goodmantheatre.org/show/theater-of-the-mind/" target="_blank" ><u>goodmantheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Matthew C. Yee’s new play with music,<b> “White Rooster,”</b> draws from a personal family story rooted in early 1900s China and reimagines an American ghost town in a tale that weaves together puppetry, movement, song and folklore. Yee directs. From March 5-April 12 at Lookingglass Theatre, The Joan & Paul Theatre, 163 E. Pearson St. Tickets: $30+. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://lookingglasstheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>lookingglasstheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Music Theater Works presents <b>“Cats,”</b> Andrew Lloyd Webber’s Tony Award-winning musical set in the world of Jellicle cats as they gather for the annual Jellicle Ball, where one will be chosen for a new life. Mandy Modic directs and choreographs. From March 5-29 at North Shore Center for the Performing Arts, 9501 Skokie Blvd., Skokie. Tickets: $19.50-$106. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://musictheaterworks.com" target="_blank" ><u>musictheaterworks.com</u></a>.</li></ul><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-790000" name="image-790000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/be7057b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+1878/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F99%2F2572ab074b2d81a7dccdcc7e1a6d%2Fthe-jackie-wilson-story-photo-by-aaron-reese-boseman.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e3be357/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+1878/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F99%2F2572ab074b2d81a7dccdcc7e1a6d%2Fthe-jackie-wilson-story-photo-by-aaron-reese-boseman.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/43d4efe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+1878/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F99%2F2572ab074b2d81a7dccdcc7e1a6d%2Fthe-jackie-wilson-story-photo-by-aaron-reese-boseman.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Jackie Wilson Story - photo by Aaron Reese Boseman.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/43d4efe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+1878/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F99%2F2572ab074b2d81a7dccdcc7e1a6d%2Fthe-jackie-wilson-story-photo-by-aaron-reese-boseman.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dfda519/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+1878/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F99%2F2572ab074b2d81a7dccdcc7e1a6d%2Fthe-jackie-wilson-story-photo-by-aaron-reese-boseman.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/43d4efe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+1878/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F81%2F99%2F2572ab074b2d81a7dccdcc7e1a6d%2Fthe-jackie-wilson-story-photo-by-aaron-reese-boseman.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jaitee Thomas stars in the titular role in “The Jackie Wilson Story.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Aaron Reese Boseman</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-fb638d84-15b7-11f1-859f-4fbd3d83f61e"><li>Black Ensemble Theater opens its 50th anniversary season with the reprisal of one of its biggest hits, <b>“The Jackie Wilson Story,”</b> which chronicles the rise and fall of the legendary R&B singer known as “Mr. Excitement.” Jaitee Thomas stars; Jackie Taylor directs. From March 7-April 26 at Black Ensemble Theater, 4450 N. Clark St. Tickets: $59-69. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://blackensemble.org" target="_blank" ><u>blackensemble.org</u></a>.</li><li>Tony Award-winning musical <b>“The Drowsy Chaperone”</b> is filled with hilarious chaos featuring showgirls, gangsters, mistaken identities and one very tipsy chaperone. L. Walter Stearns directs. From March 6-April 19 at Theo Unique Cabaret Theatre, 721 Howard St., Evanston. Tickets: $33-$66. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://theo-u.com" target="_blank" ><u>theo-u.com</u></a>.</li><li>In <b>“Flights for Future Generations,”</b> performance artist Lindsey Barlag Thornton uses movement, text, sound and imagery in an exploration of the history of women aviators and spiritualists as she navigates a fragile course between faith and uncertainty. At 7:30 p.m. March 6-7 at Steppenwolf ‘s 1700 Theater, 1700 N. Halsted St. Tickets: $23. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://steppenwolf.org/lookout" target="_blank" ><u>steppenwolf.org/lookout</u></a>.</li></ul><h3>Dance</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a60000" name="image-a60000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f7ef79/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Fbc%2F5848dd2a4621a37e7dab2cdf4944%2Fturn-it-out-with-tiler-peck-and-friends-photo-by-christopher-duggan.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/34351a1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Fbc%2F5848dd2a4621a37e7dab2cdf4944%2Fturn-it-out-with-tiler-peck-and-friends-photo-by-christopher-duggan.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8388d8e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4a%2Fbc%2F5848dd2a4621a37e7dab2cdf4944%2Fturn-it-out-with-tiler-peck-and-friends-photo-by-christopher-duggan.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Turn It Out with Tiler Peck and Friends - It’s followed by performances of Peck’s “Thousandth Orange,” Alonzo King’s “Swift Arrow” and Peck, Michelle Dorrance and Jillian Meyers “Time Spell.” At 7:30 p.m. March 7 and 3 p.m. March 8 at The Auditorium, 50 E. Ida B. Wells Dr. Tickets: $35+. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://auditoriumtheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>auditoriumtheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Chicago Tap Theatre presents <b>“The Countdown: Where Numerology Meets the Beat,”</b> a program in which artistic director Mark Yonally uses numerology as a way to play with tap. Features new choreography by Yonally, Molly Smith, Sterling Harris and others. At 7 p.m. March 5-6 and 2 p.m. March 8 ($40-$75) with a gala performance ($150-$250) at noon March 7 at Bramble Arts Loft, 5545 N. Clark St. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://chicagotaptheatre.com" target="_blank" ><u>chicagotaptheatre.com</u></a>.</li></ul><h3>Music</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-030000" name="image-030000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/edbb425/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2250x1263+0+119/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2Fae%2F467a8b064733bea849fc2de6c220%2Fjason-isbell-photo-by-christy-bush.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c3992bb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2250x1263+0+119/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2Fae%2F467a8b064733bea849fc2de6c220%2Fjason-isbell-photo-by-christy-bush.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/218aae9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2250x1263+0+119/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2Fae%2F467a8b064733bea849fc2de6c220%2Fjason-isbell-photo-by-christy-bush.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Jason Isbell - Directed and choreographed by Amber Mak. To March 28 at Marriott Theatre for Young Audiences, 10 Marriott Drive, Lincolnshire. Tickets: $20.75. Karina Patel directs. From March 7-22 at Broadway Armory Park, 5917 N. Broadway. Tickets $15-$35. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://jackalopetheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>jackalopetheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Young Grateful Dead fans (and their parents) can dance and twirl to the iconic band’s tunes when the family concert series <b>Rock and Roll Playhouse</b> cues up the music at noon March 7 at Space, 1245 Chicago Ave., Evanston. Tickets: $20.63. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://evanstonspace.com" target="_blank" ><u>evanstonspace.com</u></a>.</li></ul>
entries.author: Mary Houlihan

entries.title: White Sox are latest learning experience for manager Will Venable
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — Will Venable knew what he was taking on when he joined the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> as their new manager prior to the 2024 season. He was part of a rebuild after they set a major league record with 121 losses. Even a net gain of 19 wins last season to a 60-102 record is not where you really want to be.</p><p>“Honestly, losing is not fun,” Venable told the Sun-Times during an interview this week at the club’s Camelback Ranch spring training complex. “But it didn’t take a personal toll on me. I signed up for everything required to ensure these guys come to the ballpark prepared to play every day regardless of the previous day’s results.</p><p>“I had the energy for it. I was positive about it and excited to do it every day.”</p><p>And so, here we are again. The Sox are midway through camp with an off day Wednesday and the team is starting to take shape, kind of. It’s about three weeks before the season opener on March 26 vs. the Brewers in Milwaukee against a team that had the best record in baseball last season with the 22nd-highest payroll.</p><p>For the Sox and Venable that’s something to shoot for, but it’s going to be tough this season with a team payroll for luxury tax purposes of $101.5 million, 28th in the league. Only Cleveland and Miami are below them and the Guardians are a perennial playoff team having made the postseason three times in the past four years. Teams in the AL and NL Central seem to be able to do more with less.</p><p>“I hope we’re better than last year,” Venable said. “There’s a lot of things we’re doing internally we have confidence in. We added to our talent pool. And I think the expectations have been raised here. Now we’ve just got to go out and play the games.”</p><p>At this late date, the Sox haven’t formulated a bullpen, nor set a starting rotation. They have nine pitchers vying for a starting slot. The Opening Day starter is still a figment of Venable’s imagination, but he promises to have one by the time camp breaks on March 23.</p><p>“It’s a collaborative process,” he said.</p><p>If Venable learned anything from his first go-around in this job, it’s that it all takes incessant work. He's had an uphill climb in his learning curve as a manager.</p><p>“Oh, I learned stuff every single day,” he said. “How to deploy players. How to communicate with players. Arranging practices differently. Whatever that might be. There are a ton of different things we learned along the way that were meaningful. There’s been tons of help from baseball ops, which is what I [also] signed up for. I have a great partnership with that group and they’ve been very supportive.”</p><p>Venable has his role models to draw from. He had a nine-year big-league career as an outfielder, playing most of that for the Padres. He came up in 2008 just as Bruce Bochy left for San Francisco and the club’s reins were taken over by Bud Black. Venable played eight seasons for Black.</p><p>As a coach, he worked under Joe Maddon with the Cubs, Alex Cora with the Red Sox and ultimately joined Bochy again in Texas as associate manager when they defeated the Arizona Diamondbacks in the 2023 World Series. Then it was on to the White Sox.</p><p>He was All-Ivy League at Princeton University in baseball and basketball and is the son of Max Venable, another major league player. No one has a more complete baseball pedigree than Venable. But there have been plenty of outside influences.</p><p>“My first minor league spring training was Boch’s last year with the Padres,” Venable recalled. “Then my first big-league spring training was Buddy’s first year. Alex Cora in Boston. I got to work for Boch recently in Texas. Dave Roberts when he was coach under Buddy. All these guys were people I took a little something from.”</p><p>Keep in mind that for Venable all those were pretty much winning experiences. He’s never had to cope with something like this.</p><p></p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Barry M. Bloom

entries.title: Chicago-based construction firm Clayco launches solar energy business
entries.summary: <p>Clayco, a Chicago-based construction firm, on Wednesday launched a new business unit to focus on large solar energy and storage projects nationwide.<b> </b></p><p>Clayco Power and Energy, led by Ryan Johnson, will add nearly 1,000 employees across the U.S. by 2027 and is expected to generate about $300 million in annual revenue. </p><p>The new unit will provide design, engineering, construction and procurement for solar energy and battery storage to companies, utilities and other partners. Clayco is currently designing a solar and battery storage system on 350 acres in Manteno, about an hour south of Chicago. Construction is scheduled to start this June.</p><p>The launch of Clayco Power and Energy comes as U.S. electricity demand is projected to grow 15% to 20% by 2030, due to the rapid growth of data centers, manufacturing facilities, electrification of transportation and residential growth, the company said. Last year, 85% of all new electric generating capacity came from solar energy and battery storage, according to Clayco.</p><p>“Ultimately, solar must be part of the solution. Solar energy is the fastest type of power to bring to the market, and should be part of a multivariable approach, along with fossil fuels, natural gas and nuclear, to address America’s growing energy needs,” Clayco CEO Anthony Johnson said.</p><p>As AI and advanced technology create more demand on the U.S. energy grid, “leveraging all forms of energy, including solar, is critical to technological innovation, the U.S. economy and our long-term national security,” Johnson said.</p><p>Clayco’s announcement came even as many clean energy sectors are threatened by new federal policies that cut incentives such as tax credits. Tariffs have also increased costs and economic uncertainty.</p><p>President Donald Trump’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2025/07/03/congress-trumps-big-beautiful-bill-what-it-means" >One Big Beautiful Bill Act</a>, signed into law last year, gutted federal support for solar and wind. Many solar companies have faced challenges, such as Chicago-based <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2026/01/31/sunvest-solar-renewable-energy-bram-walter" target="_blank" >SunVest</a>.</p><p>Forecasts after the passage of new regulations showed the U.S. is on track to lose up to 55 gigawatts of solar by 2030. That includes a 14% collapse in Illinois, the nation’s 10th largest solar market, according to a September report from the Solar Energy Industries Association.</p><p>Because of Trump’s tax and spending bill, more than 830,000 clean energy jobs nationwide could be lost by 2030, a September report from the nonpartisan business group <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/energy/2025/09/24/federal-clean-energy-policies-illinois-companies" >E2</a> said.</p><p>But solar companies got a boost when Illinois last year passed the Clean and Reliable Grid Affordability Act that creates incentives for electrical grid batteries and other renewable energy. Batteries — energy storage for solar arrays — help address lapses in solar energy when there’s no sunshine.</p><p>Johnson said the launch of Clayco Power and Energy isn’t tied to “a particular piece of legislation” but is part of the company’s larger push<b> </b>into the fast-growing energy sector.</p><p>Clayco has been headquartered in the Loop since 2013. The company has more than 3,800 employees nationwide, with about 1,600 in Chicago. It earned more than $7.6 billion in annual revenue last year.</p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Judge rules companies are entitled to refunds for Trump tariffs overturned by the Supreme Court
entries.summary: <p>In a defeat for the Trump administration, a federal judge in New York ruled Wednesday that companies that paid tariffs struck down last month by the Supreme Court are due refunds.</p><p>Judge Richard Eaton of the U.S. Court of International Trade wrote that “all importers of record’’ were “entitled to benefit’’ from the Supreme Court ruling that struck down sweeping double-digit import taxes President Donald Trump imposed last year under the 1977 International Emergency Economic Powers Act.</p><p>Eaton also wrote he alone “will hear cases pertaining to the refund of IEEPA duties.’’ The ruling offers some clarity about the tariff refund process, something the Supreme Court didn't even mention in its Feb. 20 decision. </p><p>Trade lawyer Ryan Majerus, a partner at King & Spalding and a former U.S. trade official, said he expects the government to appeal or “seek a stay to buy more time for U.S. Customs to comply.″</p><p>The federal government collected more than $130 billion in the now-defunct tariffs through mid-December and could ultimately be on the hook for refunds worth $175 billion, according to calculations by the Penn Wharton Budget Model.</p><p>Eaton was ruling specifically on a case brought by Atmus Filtration, a Nashville, Tennessee, company that makes filters and other filtration products, claiming a right to a tariff refund.</p><p>On Monday, another federal court rejected the Trump administration’s attempt to slow the refund process. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit started the next phase in the refund process by sending it to New York trade court to sort out.</p><p>Now the U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency must come up with a way to process the refunds. Customs routinely refunds tariffs when there’s been some kind of error, but its system was “not designed for a mass refund,″ trade lawyer Alexis Early, partner at Bryan Cave Leighton Paisner, said. “The devil will be in the details of the administrative process.″</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/02/20/supreme-court-trump-sweeping-tariffs-john-roberts-economic-agenda" >Supreme Court strikes down Trump’s sweeping tariffs, sparking fierce pushback and vow of new levies</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/illinois-governor/2026/02/20/pritzker-illinois-refund-trump-tariffs-supreme-court-ruling" >Pritzker demands $8.6 billion for Illinois residents after Supreme Court strikes Trump tariffs</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Mae Anderson | AP

entries.title: Peer support programs in prison could help fill health care gaps, report says
entries.summary: <p>Peer support programs within prisons could be a small step toward improving Illinois’ abysmal prison health care system, a new report says.</p><p>The report from the John Howard Association, an independent Illinois prison watchdog, examined the impacts of peer support programs, which are simply opportunities for people incarcerated to help out one another.</p><p>They can be formal and organized by a prison's administration, or they can be informal gatherings initiated by people in prison, says Jenny Vollen-Katz, the group’s executive director.</p><p>"Community helping community is a really productive way to meet needs," Vollen-Katz said.</p><p>The report, <a class="Link" href="https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5beab48285ede1f7e8102102/t/69a730cf1d63bf318de6d1b5/1772564687239/JHA+Peer+Support+Report+March+2026.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>published Wednesday</u></a>, looked at formal and informal peer support groups in correctional systems in Illinois, Wisconsin and Colorado that focus on a range of issues: mental health, substance abuse, palliative care, doula support and trauma recovery. Some provide job training and participants end up with certifications that could benefit them when they get out of prison.</p><p>"Not only is there an enormous need for care, but also a need for productive programming and the opportunity to learn skills that people can use to improve their daily lives while incarcerated," the report reads.</p><p>Peer support programs can also help fill critical gaps in medical care, something the Illinois Department of Corrections and prisons across the country struggle with, Vollen-Katz said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/02/28/health-care-illinois-prisons-consent-decree" ><u>A recent Sun-Times investigation</u></a> that the state’s critically understaffed facilities struggle to deliver proper correctional medicine to the tens of thousands of inmates in their care. And that's despite being under a consent decree, or court-enforceable settlement agreement, to improve health care since 2019.</p><p>Reports from an independent court-appointed monitor have described staffing levels as "dangerously low." For example, the department employs the equivalent of only 16 full-time physicians across 29 correctional facilities. That’s for more than 30,000 people in the state prison system.</p><p>While peer support programs cannot alone address staffing shortages, Vollen-Katz says, they could provide help in several ways. For example, people incarcerated could be trained on some basic medical practices, like taking blood pressure, drawing blood and administering vaccines.</p><p>"When you look at health care in prisons, it is huge and broken and expensive, and … there are answers, but they require investment, time, energy, money — and it is hard to compel the state to get there," Vollen-Katz said.</p><p>"Where we can find the lower lifts — where we don't have to employ more people, where there are bodies ready, willing and able to help other people to pitch in and get the work done, we need to think about how we can make that happen."</p><p>Vollen-Katz said she's heard from both people incarcerated and prison officials who are supportive and eager to work on these programs.</p><p>"I've actually been really heartened to hear that there are folks at IDOC who are interested in this, who are looking at possibilities," she said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/02/28/health-care-illinois-prisons-consent-decree" target="_blank" >Illinois prisons were ordered to improve health care for inmates. They’ve spent seven years failing.</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Cubs catcher Moisés Ballesteros goes on ABS hot streak vs. Brewers
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> catcher <b>Moisés</b> <b>Ballesteros</b> was on a hot streak Wednesday. Not hitting, but in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/02/14/could-the-cubs-use-the-new-abs-challenge-system-to-their-advantage-in-2026" >ABS challenges</a>.</p><p>From behind the plate in the Cubs' 4-1 win against the Brewers, Ballesteros won three straight challenges — two in the same at-bat against Brewers switch hitter <b>Luis Lara</b>.</p><p>“The visibility he has is incredible,” right-hander <b>Edward Cabrera</b> said, through team interpreter Fredy Quevedo Jr., after throwing 2⅔ scoreless innings. “So I'm really happy for him. I was shocked … he got three correct. Good for him. And then just to be able to have that confidence to be able to make those calls.”</p><p>In the second inning, in a 0-1 count, Cabrera<b> </b>clipped the inside edge of the strike zone with a changeup. Home plate umpire <b>Evin Johnson</b> called it a ball, but Ballesteros had that overturned for strike two.</p><p>Lara fouled off the next two pitches. Then Cabrera landed a curveball that barely grazed the outside edge of the zone. The ball call was overturned to complete the strikeout.</p><p>For good measure, two batters later, Ballesteros also won an ABS challenge on a low and outside changeup that swiped the bottom of the zone.</p><p>A fourth-inning heat check ended Ballesteros’ streak. An ABS review upheld the ball call on an inside cutter that Cubs right-hander <b>Jaxon Wiggins</b> threw to Brewers left-handed hitter <b>David Hamilton</b>.</p><p>Ballesteros experienced ABS in the minors, but he said it’s something he’s been working on this spring as well.</p><p>The Cubs plan to have Ballesteros play more behind the plate than last year, when he almost exclusively served as a designated hitter during his major-league stints.</p><p>“I feel really confident right now behind the plate,” he said Wednesday. “And I trust myself behind the plate right now.”</p><h3>Counsell reflection on Horton</h3><p>Cubs right-hander <b>Cade Horton</b> has yet to allow a run in two spring appearances. In three innings total, he’s given up just one hit. It’s been a strong start to camp, after a glowing rookie 2025 season.</p><p>Expectations are high for Horton’s sophomore season, but manager <b>Craig Counsell</b> pushed back on the notion that he’s only just starting to scratch the surface.</p><p>“Cade was about as good as you're going to see a pitcher pitch last year,” Counsell said Wednesday morning. “Growth from that season? I don't think you're ever going to expect more than that. If he would have racked up 50 more innings, that's a Cy Young candidate.</p><p>“We're looking forward to giving him the ball and getting a full season from him. But last year was pretty remarkable.”</p><h3>Checking in from Team USA</h3><p>Cubs left-hander <b>Matthew Boyd</b> stopped by the Cubs complex after throwing two scoreless innings for Team USA on Wednesday in an exhibition game against the San Francisco Giants, Counsell said.</p><p>“To be around those players is every player's dream,” Counsell said of the star-studded United States national team. “Matt's been around, and he said it gives you different feelings walking into the clubhouse. And that's why you do it, right? This is one of the elder statesmen, in terms of age of the team. So it's just special. It's just special when you get in the room with the best in the world, that's a different feeling.”</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3
